part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.mission_and_vision:
Our Mission & Vision 
We do a lot of silly things at camp, but the underlying reason we are here is serious business.  
Mission Statement: The mission of Scouting America is to prepare young people to make ethical and 
moral choices over their lifetimes by instilling in them the values of the Scout Oath and Law.  
Vision Statement: Scouting America will prepare every eligible youth in America to become a responsible, 
participating citizen and leader who is guided by the Scout Oath and Law. 
Everything we do is guided by the Scout Oath (Duty to God and country, duty to others, duty to self) and 
the Scout Law (A Scout is Trustworthy, Loyal, Helpful, Friendly, Courteous, Kind, Obedient, Cheerful, Thrifty, 
Brave, Clean, and Reverent). If your actions align with these principles, you're on the right track.
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.core_pillars_of_summer_camp:
The Core Pillars of Summer Camp  
An organized camping program like ours is an educational powerhouse that targets growth in four specific 
areas: 
●​
Physical: Camp helps develop habits of healthy living through balanced meals and supervised 
physical activities. 
●​
Mental: Advancement programs offer challenges that build self-sufficiency and responsibility. 
●​
Social: The community of a summer camp helps build strong, morally rich people skills. 
●​
Spiritual: Spiritually rich time in nature helps develop a deeper understanding of a scout’s place in 
the universe.

part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.what_makes_a_staff:
WHAT MAKES A STAFF?  
A Camp Staff is more than a group of employees going about their assigned duties. The staff is a group of 
dedicated Scouts and Scouters who lead by example, set the tone for the attitude and excitement in camp, 
and are responsible for the lifetime of memories made during their weeks at camp.  
To get a good idea of what we are asking you to do, please pay close attention to the following four areas 
which will influence our Scouts during their visit to Camp Lawton:  
Appearance; Hygiene, uniform, and how you present yourself make an  
impression. Before you speak, your appearance will have spoken.  
 Attitude; Stay positive and remember you are there to serve as well as  
have fun. Try to have fun serving.  
 Personality: Patience, friendliness, humor, respect and enthusiasm;  
these traits make all the diƯerence.  
 Knowledge: When teaching a Merit Badge or skill, the staƯ member should be an authority on the subject 
that he or she is teaching. In addition, the staƯ should be well versed in the structure and operations of 
Camp Lawton.  
Remember, our goal is to provide a safe, educational, and fun environment for our Scouts. You play a major 
role in meeting those goals.
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.chain_of_command:
The Chain of Command 
The chain of command is just a flowchart of who answers to who.  
●​
The Camp Director is the prime authority in camp.  
●​
Under the Camp Director is the Program Director and the structural core of camp: the Ranger, 
Trading Post Manager/Business Manager, Medic, Kitchen Staff, and Camp Commissioner.  
●​
All of the Program Staff (statistically, that’ll probably be you) fall under the Program Director.  
●​
Reporting directly to the Program Director are the Area Directors.  
●​
Reporting to the Area Directors are the rest of the Program Staff (Instructors, Junior Staff, 
CITs).  
 
 
Put in a more practical way, there are two branches of the camp structure that matter to you: 
1.​
The Camp Director runs the camp, making sure things are done safely, efficiently, on budget, and 
according to national standards. It’s their job to make sure everyone gets fed, the facilities are kept 
maintained, no one dies, and we don’t get shut down.  
6 

 
2.​ The Program Director is in charge of the camp experience - the merit badge and advancement 
classes, campfires, Funday, and the general camp staff.  
If you like, the Program Director handles everything “on stage” and the Camp Director handles everything 
“back stage.”  
 
Does that mean you don't have to do anything the Ranger or Commissioner asks you to do? Absolutely not. 
If they're asking you to do something, there's probably a reason. Generally speaking, unless it directly 
conflicts with your specific duties or your personal ethics or morality or if it’s illegal, go ahead and do it. If it 
does conflict, talk to your immediate director to figure out what should take priority. Don’t say no to 
anything without a good reason.  
 
It should go without saying, health and safety emergencies will always take priority over any other 
duty. 
 
Also, as we’ll say repeatedly throughout this document, we have an open door policy. ANY issue, concern, 
grievance, or personal concerns can and should be brought to the administration and we will do our best to 
address it immediately. If you feel the need, you are also welcome to take your concerns directly to Catalina 
Council or the Council Executive Board.  
​
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.age_requirements_for_staff_leadership:
Age Requirements for Staff Leadership  
To comply with National Camp Accreditation Program (NCAP) and state labor guidelines, certain roles carry 
strict minimum age requirements. 
●​
Camp Director & Program Director: Minimum age 21 
●​
Range Activities Director and Climbing Director: Minimum age 21.  
●​
Area Directors: Minimum age 18 
●​
Instructors / Junior Staff: Minimum age 16 
●​
Counselors in Training (CIT): Ages 14-15.   
CITs 
Counselors in Training are in a unique position. They are considered part of a training program, kind of like 
interns. Labor laws limit the time 14 and 15 year olds can work (7am to 9pm and limited to 8 hours). It is 
everyone's job to make sure they are treated right. Not as “children”, but not as “employees.” They are our 
next generation of staff. They're not go-fers (“go for this, go for that”) or clean up servants, but nor should 
they be left to teach a merit badge by themselves.  
 
Counselors-in-Training (CIT) and Volunteers 
You will also see volunteer staff and CITs joining us for short-term rotations throughout the summer. These 
individuals are staff members, full stop. They are to be treated with the same professionalism and respect 
as any other colleague. To our campers and visitors, the distinction between a volunteer and a full-season 
staffer should be invisible. 
The CIT program provides an excellent way for future leaders to explore different departments and for 
camp leadership to identify where their talents shine brightest. By the end of their stay, CITs walk away with 
a deep appreciation for the effort required to keep the mountain running. They are incredibly valuable to 
our mission today and represent the future of our staff legacy. 
 
Junior Staff 
As older teenagers, this qualifies as a summer job. Lower paying than the alternatives, but far more fulfilling. 
Studies have found that summer camp work is a far better preparation for excelling at any workforce than 
those who just worked retail or food service. They learn interpersonal skills, teaching, food service, 
cleaning, professional behavior, procedural adherence, how to work through the toughest conditions… The 
list goes on. To hiring managers who know, a history of camp staff is a gold mine for new hires.  
 
Adult Staff 
7 

 
It may surprise you to learn that Adult Staff are anyone 18 and older. Most are likely to be Area Directors, but 
not all.

National Camp School is a great week-long program that teaches participants everything they need to 
know to run a high quality, accreditation level camp. Certifications are good for 5 years. Attendance is 
usually paid for by Catalina Council. Who goes and what arrangements are made are handled by the 
Catalina Council and the Camp Director.  
 
 
 
 
What are my duties anyway? 
 
I'm so glad you asked! Camp Staff is much more than a regular job. So much so that calling it a “job” is 
woefully insufficient. It's a lifestyle. A calling. In the coming weeks, you will make friends, maybe enemies, 
8 

 
probably a few frienemies. Heck, you might even fall in love (see the section on Fraternization)! You'll do 
things you've never done and things you never thought you could do. You'll do tedious stuff, gross stuff, 
grueling stuff and painful stuff. It'll be really hard at times. But I promise, you can do it and it’ll be worth it. 
Afterward.   
 
Here is a general list of what we expect of you (unless special arrangements are made with the 
Camp Director): 
●​
Attend all staff meetings, trainings, and Staff Week.  
●​
Engage in these meetings and trainings, offering your ideas for the continued improvement of 
camp. All ideas are valuable, no matter where you are on the chain. 
●​
Be early to things, not just on time. The classic rule of thumb is “if you’re not five minutes early, 
you’re late.” 
●​
Participate in and assist with all camp-wide programs such as flags, meals, campfires, and games.  
●​
Be courteous and respectful to everyone, especially campers and visitors.  
●​
Follow the chain of command. Your Area Director is your supervisor and your point person for any 
concerns. Area Directors will then go to the Program Director who then speaks to the Camp 
Director. That’s not to say, “you should never dare speak to the mysterious and god-like camp 
administration,” but a clear communication structure helps ensure the right information flows 
through the right channels.  
●​
Keep your appearance presentable and respectable. Shave, brush your hair, shower regularly, wear 
deodorant, brush your teeth, change your clothes on a regular basis (you probably won’t have 7 
days worth of Class A and B uniforms to change into every day, but have backup options in case 
one gets dirty), and get haircuts on the weekends if needed and possible. Put simply, don’t look like 
a slob and for the sake of your cabin-mates, try not to stink.  
●​
Be in appropriate uniform for all activities. See the
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.glossary:
Glossary section below for the uniform 
standards. That means Class A/Field uniform for Flags, campfires, chapel services, and other formal 
activities. Class B/Activity uniform for daily program. “Civvies” (casual wear) can be worn on Staff 
Hill out of sight of campers, but all clothing must be scout appropriate (we’re not prudes, but if your 
grandmother would question your choices, it may not be the best choice for camp). Keep in mind 
that you may have to change again on short notice should something come up. Your down-time 
cannot always be guaranteed. If you are off of Staff Hill and not in at least Class B, you will be asked 
to go change immediately.  
●​
Maintain your readiness and willingness to perform your duties by eating properly and getting 
adequate sleep. This may sound like something that should go without saying, but it's the most 
common problem we have with staff members (including the admin team).  
●​
Keep your staff quarters clean, orderly, and comfortable for all residents. We do not plan to have 
cabin inspections unless it becomes necessary. Don’t make it necessary. 
●​
Take proper care of all camp equipment and facilities. 
●​
Be mindful that the primary responsibility of each staff member is to be of service to the Scouts, 
camp, and each other.  
●​
Take personal responsibility for the success of the entire camp. Even if it’s not in your job 
description, there’s always something you can do to make it better.  
●​
Name Tags, indicating that you are Camp Lawton 2026 Staff,  must be visible at all times. It should 
be on the top half of your body, either hung on a lanyard around your neck or clipped to your shirt.  
●​
Abide by the Scout Oath, Law, and ALL of the ideals of scouting. 
 
Other duties as assigned… The list above is by no means a comprehensive list of what your duties will be 
over the summer. Covering everything would make this a much longer book and would still likely miss some 
of the more unpredictable possibilities. Just be prepared to do whatever it takes to make this summer at 
Camp Lawton a success. 
 
9 

 
What can we expect from the Camp Administration? 
We’re asking a lot of you there. What kind of monsters would we be if we expected so much of you but 
didn't tell you what to expect from us? There's a whole legal section later that'll cover our responsibility to 
you as your employers, but let's talk about what we owe you as human people. 
●​
The staff is a family (figuratively). Family doesn't mean we like each other all the time, but we're 
always there for each other. We’re not your parents, but we promise to be the grown ups you need 
when you need us.  
●​
Within the bounds of Scouting America's Safeguarding Youth guidelines (which we’ll go into plenty 
later on), you should always feel safe to talk to anyone in leadership about anything that's on your 
mind.  
●​
Camp Lawton is a safe space. We owe you a home where you feel free from unfair expectations, 
bullying, harassment, abuse, and pranks. 
●​
While discipline is necessary for effective management, people make mistakes. We will not punish 
you unreasonably and most lapses in judgement will be allowed a second chance. Serious violations 
of camp guidelines, however, will be handled with the severity befitting the act.  
●​
Reasonable and prompt compensation for your work will be provided according to position and 
employment agreement. “Compensation” doesn’t always mean money, but your hard work 
deserves to be properly appreciated.  
 
 
10
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.the_rules:
The Rules 
The Junior Staff Conundrum 
Under ideal conditions, the vast majority of Camp Lawton's 
staff is made up of Junior Staff and CITs between 14 and 17. That 
of course presents this chilling challenge: we've got a bunch of 
hormonal mixed gender teenagers running this place! How are 
we ever to survive?  
Later on, we'll have a Code of Conduct for you to sign with all 
the official rules for you to follow, but let's just address a few 
things with the blunt honest maturity I trust you can handle. 
 
 
 
Phones 
I'm not going to be one of those people who blames all of society's woes on kids who won't look up from 
their smartphones. You've got lives on there. I get it. However, Camp Lawton needs you here if we're going 
to get what we need from you, and if you're going to get anything out of the summer yourself. Keep it put 
away. That goes for tablets and laptops as well (unless they’re approved for use as a program aid). We will 
begin the summer with no blanket ban on phones, but any abuse and we will have to drop the hammer.  
Photography and social media 
While we’re at it, we encourage taking photographs and videos of camp. Ideally, use a dedicated camera 
device so we don’t run into any phone abuse issues. However, the sharing of scouts’ faces on social media 
can get really messy. Run any content you intend to post to social media by the Camp Director. It’s not 
censorship, we just don’t want to put any kids in harm’s way.  
Video Games and other recreation 
Camp can be stressful and we all need a healthy release. In your limited off time, video games can be great 
for that. So can books, music, movies, TV, even card and board games. If you want to start up a D&D 
campaign, I might even join you!  
However… we're gonna have a problem if:  
1.​
You're too loud and are heard by scouts or leaders. 
It doesn't take much. 
2.​
You are late to or miss a program because of it.  
3.​
You are playing, watching, or doing something not 
in line with scout values. That means M rated 
games, R rated movies, card games with excessive 
language, violence or sexual content, etc. Cards 
Against Humanity is fun, but stick to Apples To 
Apples. 
And leave your huge gaming rigs at home! Yes, I’ve seen it, and it’s embarrassing. 
 
11 

 
 
Drugs, Alcohol, Pornography  
No, no, and no. Duh. If you don't know why, this may not be the right place 
for you. Yes, prescription drugs are fine as long as they're registered with the 
medic. No, I'm not going to debate with you what qualifies as pornography. 
And don't give me any “but weed isn't an addictive drug” or “it's non-alcoholic 
beer.” This is a Scout camp! Binge on Mountain Dew and sour gummy worms 
like the rest of us! 
 
 
Fraternization  
We’ll be getting to SYT guidelines in Part 2, but let’s talk about 
something more inevitable. Over the summer, you’ll develop 
close bonds with your fellow staff. Sometimes very close. I've 
been there myself. Whatever your “trapped 
together/coming-of-age YA romance” love story may be, keep 
the physical stuff out of camp. Why?  
1.​
Morality! You're Scouts. On staff, you have the 
responsibility of representing the pinnacle of scouting 
values, which don’t include PDA.    ​
 
2.​
The Birds and the Bees! Holding hands leads to kissing. 
Kissing leads to French kissing. Yadda-yadda-yadda. And 
that's how babies are made. Let's not please.  
3.​
Drama! Sure, smooching is fun! But you're stuck here 
for the season with these people. If something goes 
wrong, now we've all got to suffer.  
4.​
Professionalism! Fraternization can put us on the path 
to harassment, abuse, or assault complaints. That means 
people getting fired, lawsuits, civil or criminal trials, and 
entirely too much paperwork. Trust me, no one wants 
any of that and you getting handsy at camp just isn't worth it.  
Sound fair? To be clear, I wouldn’t dream of getting in the way of your personal awakenings, but as you’ll 
learn in the Safeguarding Youth Training, there is zero tolerance for some of this stuff and I want to make 
sure you’re safe from any of that.  
 
12
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.stress_management:
Stress Management and Mental Stability 
“At some point, everything’s gonna go south and you’re going to say, this is it. This is how I end. Now, you 
can either accept that, or you can get to work. That’s all it is. You just begin. You do the math. You solve one 
problem and you solve the next one and then the next. And if you solve enough problems, you get to come 
home.” - The Martian (2015), screenwriter Drew Goddard 
 
Let's be honest: staffing is HARD. Feeling stressed, overwhelmed, cranky, or even a little depressed at camp 
doesn't mean you are failing. It means your brain is reacting normally to a highly unnatural, high-stimulus 
environment. You are being tasked with managing the safety, education, and entertainment of dozens of 
younger kids with very little time for yourself. That carries a massive cognitive load. We ask a lot of you, but 
not that.   
 
Here is how you survive the summer with your sanity intact: 
 
1. Work the Problem 
When the stress hits, it’s easy to spiral into catastrophic thinking ("I can't do this," "Everyone hates me," "This 
summer is awful"). Stop and work the problem. Run a self-diagnostic: 
●​
Are you dehydrated? Dehydration mimics anxiety, spiking your heart rate and making you irritable. 
Remember your Water Appreciation Moments (WAM). 
●​
Are you fueled? Surviving solely on energy drinks and sour gummy worms will crash your system. 
Eat actual food at the Dining Hall. 
●​
Are you sleep-deprived? Getting enough sleep can be tricky at camp when you’ve got a lot on your 
mind and with a cabin full of other people with a lot on theirs. Lights Out isn’t just an arbitrary rule. 
It’s there to give you ample opportunity to get the sleep you need. Use that time from 10pm to 6am 
for sleeping!   
2. Use Your Siesta Science has consistently confirmed that napping for a short time each day improves 
mental health and productivity. The daily schedule intentionally builds in a "Siesta" gap between lunch and 
the afternoon program. It is tempting to use that time for all the stuff you've been meaning to get done, but 
it is literally a “nap time” set aside as time for staff, scouts, and leaders to reboot. Go to your cabin, lie down, 
and let your brain stop processing external input for an hour. You don't have to sleep, but you do need to 
relax. 
3. Manage Sensory Overload Camp is loud. Between the incessant chatter of preteen kids, the cheering 
on The Logs, and living in a cabin with fellow staff, you rarely experience true silence. If you feel yourself 
getting overstimulated, find a quiet space out of sight of the campers where you can safely decompress. 
Listen to the breeze in the trees. Give your nervous system a chance to return to baseline. Just remember 
your Area Director needs to know where you are and if you're under 18, you do still need a buddy. Duct tape 
is available if your buddy needs some encouragement to silently enjoy the gentle sounds of the forest. (For 
legal reasons, that’s a joke.) 
4. Tag Out (The Strategic Retreat) If a scout is pushing your buttons, a lesson is completely derailing, or 
you feel a meltdown coming on, do not force yourself to power through until you snap. Tell your Area 
Director, "I need five minutes." Going somewhere “away” and taking a few deep breaths and resetting is a 
hallmark of professional competence, not a sign of weakness. 
5. Call in the Grown-Ups If your mental health is slipping, if you are experiencing severe homesickness, or 
if a situation with a camper or cabinmate is too heavy for you to carry alone, go to an adult staff member 
that you trust. You are never expected to go through anything alone. Please don’t suffer in silence.   
 
A reality check 
13 

 
As dangerous as it is to get overworked and burned out, it can also be all too easy to say “the Staff 
Handbook says I should prioritize my mental health, so I'm going to just stay in bed all week.” It happens to 
even the strongest of us and it's easy to spiral into a lazy depression. As miserable as you may be, don't give 
up on your job. Talk to people. Cling to your purpose. Hiding from your responsibilities is never going to be 
the answer. Believe me, it just makes you feel worse. If you really need some time off, talk to the 
administration. We’ll work something out.  
 
14 

 
Glossary 
 
Class A / Field Uniform:  
●​
An official Scout uniform shirt - this can be Scouts BSA, Explorer, Venture, Sea Scouts, or an 
international Scouting organization you’re registered with 
●​
Scout pants/shorts - NOT jeans! They must be official uniform bottoms (Scouts BSA is olive green 
for instance, Venture is a grey). Officially licenced skirts or other types of leg coverings are also 
acceptable. As the weather varies in camp, both shorts and pants are advisable.  
●​
Belt - A scouting approved belt, whether an officially branded belt or a leather belt with a scouting 
related belt buckle, as long as it’s appropriate and indicates scouting in some way.  
●​
Closed toed shoes - We recommend hiking boots or reliable sneakers that will hold up to significant 
abuse on the rough trails of camp. Closed toed shoes must be worn at all times in camp unless in 
your cabin or taking a shower (in which case we advise water shoes, flip flops, or crocs to prevent 
fungal outbreaks). 
●​
Socks - We will not be picky about official Scout socks, but you should be wearing socks of some 
kind.   
●​
An undershirt - While optional, the varying weather of the mountain makes layering our friend. The 
easiest undershirt is your staff shirt, making it easy to switch between Class A and Class B.   
●​
Hat (optional) - if worn with the Class A, it must be scout related in some way. 
 
Class B / Activity Uniform: 
●​
Camp Lawton Staff Shirt - Primarily this year’s staff shirt, but past year staff shirts are acceptable. If 
necessary, any Camp Lawton shirt may be worn with administration approval. Other scouting 
related shirts may be approved by the Camp or Program Director.  
●​
Scout pants/shorts - See above. 
●​
Closed toed shoes - See above. 
●​
Socks - See above.  
●​
Hat (optional) - Must be camp appropriate, but keeping the sun off your head and neck is an 
excellent idea. 
●​
Water Bottle - It’s not enough to rely on drinking fountains around camp. You must have a water 
container on you at all times. This is part of your uniform.  
 
The Logs: The bleacher seating in front of the Dining Hall was, for a century, made of logs. Though they 
have received a substantial upgrade in favor of aesthetics and comfort, we still call them The Logs out of 
tradition. This is where scouts will gather for meals and staff will lead songs and activities while they wait.  
 
WAM: Water Appreciation Moment. If heard, everyone within earshot must take a drink of water. This is 
because dehydration is a very real problem in camp and drinking water should be aggressively encouraged 
among both staff and scouts.  
 
Troop Friend: Youth staff will be assigned to act as Troop Friends for assigned troops. The Troop Friend will 
act as the unit’s guide on Sunday, a liaison and friend of the troop throughout the week, and inspect the 
campsite before the unit leaves on Sunday.  You will have the opportunity to make friends, inspire young 
scouts, and perhaps guide Scouts in the direction of becoming camp staff themselves. Troop Friends are 
not expected to spend all their spare time with their troop; just make an appearance now and then. It makes 
a bigger difference than you think.   
 
Staff Hill: The hill on the north end of camp designated for Staff living facilities.   
 
Smellables: Anything with an odor or scent that might attract wildlife. All Smellables must be secured in the 
Smellables Shed by the youth shower house.   
 
 
15
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.this_is_your_life_schedule:
This Is Your Life 
“Seven A.M., the usual morning lineup 
Start on the chores and sweep 'til the floor's all clean 
Polish and wax, do laundry, and mop and shine up 
Sweep again, and by then it's like 7:15” - Rapunzel, Disney's Tangled  
 
The following is a rough schedule of what to expect from your time during each week of camp. Times and 
programs are liable to change summer to summer, and even week to week, so take the specifics here with a 
grain of salt. We’ll give you more accurate schedules as camp approaches.  
 
Sunday 
All staff are expected to report to camp by 12pm on Sunday (the Camp Director may change this as 
necessary). You'll need to sign in at the office and get settled. Be in Class A uniform in time for the 
meeting at 1pm. You’ll be in Class A uniform all day. Scouts will start arriving at 2pm. Junior Staff will 
each be assigned a troop, to which you will be the Troop Friend. You will guide them through the check-in 
process and to their campsite. You'll then act as, well, the troop’s friend and liaison. You'll want to visit with 
them at least once a day through the week to see how they're doing, pass any problems they report to the 
appropriate people, and generally get them to like you. It's an easy way to get named in Troop Evals at the 
end of the week!  
The check-in process is fairly simple:  
1.​
Camp Administration, the Medic, and Troop Friends will be meeting them at the entry gate next to 
the parking lot. While you’re waiting for an assignment, help act as parking attendants, making sure 
everyone backs into their parking spots, no one rolls down the hill or hits a tree, and make sure no 
one gets run over.  
2.​
The Camp and Program Director will check the troop in, making sure all of their paperwork is ready 
to go.  
3.​
The Medic will check the troop’s medical forms, asking questions regarding any special medical 
needs and checking in all medications.  
4.​ When check-in is complete, the Troop Friend will guide the troop to their campsite, have them drop 
their stuff (don’t start setting up yet).  
5.​ Then the Troop Friend will give the troop a camp tour, making sure they know where the Flag 
Grounds are, Health Lodge, Dining Hall, and program areas are. Try to visit every site as important 
information may need to be relayed by the directors in those areas.  
6.​ Then the troop can return to their campsite, start setting up, and they’ll meet for flags that evening.  
 
There is a short Scouts Own chapel service which is encouraged for both scouts and staff, but it is optional 
based on your personal faith.  
Then Flags and Dinner. After dinner there'll be a Scoutmaster meeting discussing how the schedule will 
work and making sure scouts are registered to get the badges they want. Area Directors will need to be 
there, but the rest of you are off the hook for the night.  
 
Daily Life 
●​
6am is the Eagle Trail, an inspirational hike up Eagle Hill. You’re encouraged to attend with your 
friend troop, but it is optional.   
●​
Flags is Class A, of course. Be lined up early! This is a short flag ceremony and announcements.  
●​
Immediately following dismissal from flags is breakfast. The troops should sit on the Logs and staff 
(determined by a duty roster) will engage with them until the troops are called to line up for food. 
Energetic songs are strongly encouraged in the morning, but it’s valuable to keep things diverse.  
●​
During meals, staff are expected to sit with the campers. Chat, laugh, socialize. Be their friend. 
Remember that our camp’s greatest asset is the ability for the scouts to develop meaningful bonds 
and friendships with the staff.  
16 

 
●​
One troop and one staff member will be assigned to “KP duty” for each meal. You'll report to the 
Kitchen Steward (a staff member assigned to the kitchen for this purpose) for instructions.  
●​
Program time is between 9 and 11:50. Be in your area ready to teach by 9. You should already have 
the supplies you’ll need for your class ready to go.  
●​
Lunch is at noon, followed by a “Siesta” period. It's literally nap time. Relax! These are long days. 
Make use of your down time to refresh. Trust me.  
●​
Then more program from 2-5 pm. 
●​
Flags and dinner work much the same way as breakfast.  
 
Meetings 
Every day there will be a short meeting (either before flags in the morning or after program at night at the 
discretion of the Camp and/or Program Director) to debrief on the previous day and prepare for the coming 
day. Meetings may also be called periodically at other times in the week if something needs to be 
addressed more urgently or in more depth.   
 
Staff Campfire 
Monday night is the Staff Campfire. We'll be planning it during staff week, so bring some pitches! Check 
out the advanced comedy training in Part 1 and the Songbook in Part 3.  
 
Funday 
Wednesday has always been the hump day when homesick kids feel like they can't take it anymore and the 
merit badge junkies are overwhelmed. So we took the national standard requiring a “campwide game” and 
made it a whole day! Some of it has a clear purpose, developing traditional scout skills. Some activities are 
just fun nonsense (don't tell the scoutmasters that). The specific activities will change every year, so we’ll 
get into it during the in-person training meetings.  
 
Camper Campfire 
Over the week, the Program Director will be working with the SPLs to plan the Friday night Camper 
Campfire. The camp staff usually throw in a few bits, but for the most part it’s the camper’s turn to entertain 
you. That may either be a reward or punishment, accordingly.  
 
Saturday  
Whew! What a week, huh? The whole day is designed to get the Scouts OUT so we can go home. The flag 
ceremony is truncated, breakfast is simple, and no programs are scheduled. You will be returning to your 
Troop Friend duties in which you'll inspect the campsite and clear them to leave. After EVERY troop has left, 
we'll have our meeting, go over troop evaluations, reward the most mentioned person(s), and make some 
final announcements before setting you loose to terrorize Tucson for 24 hours.  
17
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.customer_service:
Customer Service 
One of the most important parts of your job on camp staff is making scouts feel appreciated and 
encouraging an atmosphere where scouts can thrive. In fact, Camp Lawton’s most powerful asset is its size 
and staff. Ask any scout who has gone to a larger camp to name five staff members. They won’t be able to. 
That’s because at other camps, staff are just there to deliver the program. A camp our size allows us to build 
personal relationships with the scouts and give them unique experiences they won’t get anywhere else. To 
help you understand your role, here’s a few conceptual methods to follow. Much of it is directed more 
toward management, but it’s helpful for you to understand it as well.   
Dennis Snow’s customer service tips from Disney 
1.​
Hire for Attitude: Instead of hiring based solely on skills and trying to train personality later, 
prioritize hiring naturally friendly people and then train them on the necessary skills. 
2.​
Everything Speaks: Every detail, from the cleanliness of your facility to how quickly you respond to 
a request, sends a message to your scouts and their leaders about how much you value them. You 
must own every aspect of the experience. 
3.​
It's a Stage: Treat every employee as a cast member and every job as a performance. Maintain a 
clear distinction between the "onstage" experience for the customer and the "backstage" 
operations that should never be visible to them. 
4.​
Map First: Before training, map out every touchpoint through the lens of the customer. Identifying 
these points allows you to prevent service breakdowns before they occur. 
5.​
Prioritize Systems over Scripts: Excellence should be built into repeatable, reliable systems that 
anyone can follow, rather than relying on individual heroics or rigid, unnatural scripts. 
 
FISH Philosophy  
 
1.​
Play: Bring a lighthearted, spontaneous energy to your environment. This isn't about ignoring your 
responsibilities or enforcing “mandatory workplace fun.” It’s about finding creativity and joy in 
routine tasks. The work still gets done, but the atmosphere feels less rigid. 
2.​
Make Their Day: Look for ways to create a memorable, positive experience for someone else. It 
doesn’t require massive grand gestures or giving away free products. It is usually a small, 
unexpected interaction - remembering a name, sharing a genuine laugh, or sincerely validating a 
customer's frustration. 
3.​
Be There: Give the customer (or your coworkers) your complete, undivided attention. This is often 
the hardest step to maintain. It means putting away distractions, pausing your mental to-do list, and 
actively listening. You are treating the person in front of you as the only thing that matters at that 
moment.  
4.​
Choose Your Attitude: Take total responsibility for the mindset you bring to work each day.You 
can't control angry customers, bad weather, or system outages. But you can decide how you react 
to them. It is an active, daily choice you make before you walk through the door, not a permanent 
personality trait. 
 
 
18
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.program_areas:
Program Areas 
Merit Badges are taught in organized program areas. Each area is composed of an Area Director and 
Instructors. 
 
The Area Director 
Each program area has an Area Director. The Area Director is responsible for the upkeep and operation of 
his/her area with this help of the instructor staff assigned to the area.  
.  
Making Your Area Appealing 
The Area Director can make an area popular and effective by: 
●​
Making a list of what the area needs throughout each week.   
●​
Making do with what you have. 
●​
Securing all equipment after operating hours.  
●​
Developing a system for use of equipment.  
●​
Keeping your area clean and aesthetically pleasing at all times.  
●​
Becoming an expert at your subject matter.  
●​
Making learning fun with games and hands-on activities. 
●​
Knowing interesting things about what you are teaching that isn't in the book.  
●​
Using gimmicks, special events, and contests.  
●​
Keeping it simple and making it fun.  
●​
Making your area something special and creating a reason people want to come. 
 
Lesson Plans 
Area Directors are responsible for preparing lesson plans for each Merit Badge or skill offered in their areas 
prior to beginning of Staff Week. Successful lesson planning includes: 
●​
Using official Scout publications 
●​
Dividing the subject matter or requirements appropriately into the number of sessions you will be 
teaching. 
●​
Finding the most active, interesting, and engaging  methods to teach for the subject being taught. 
●​
Spending the first few minutes each day reviewing the material from the previous day. 
●​
Having a main theme or idea for each day's learning. 
●​
Setting objectives for each lesson and communicating these to the learner. 
●​
Setting specific days when projects or assignments are due. 
●​
Making sure your lessons follow a continuum from foundational material to more complex material. 
●​
Using the tools available in your area to keep your students interested from beginning to end. 
●​
Requiring no more or no less than the requirement states. 
 
Inventory 
For countless reasons, keeping accurate track of your inventory is crucial. This will be one of the first 
projects during Staff Week. Ideally we’ll have last year’s inventory to compare it to, but either way you’ll 
want to identify any supplies you are needing as quickly as possible so they can be ordered. It’s not a bad 
idea to arrange to come up to camp early to identify your needs. Keep track throughout the summer and 
you’ll need to do the inventory again at the end of the summer. Good documentation will make it easier to 
have the supplies you need next year ready to go early.   
 
Safety 
The Maintenance Committee has spent many hours preparing the facilities for summer camp, anticipating 
any needs or possible hazards. However, the Area Directors are responsible for carefully examining their 
program area for any potential hazards. Identified hazards are marked by the director and the management 
of the hazard is established based upon the recommendation of the Area Director, Camp Director and 
Camp Ranger. Hazards can include trail hazards, dangerous trees, bad fencing and other nuisances. Also 
consider any aesthetic problems in the area (such as trash piles and ugly or broken tables) and fix them 
accordingly. 
 
Instructors 
19 

 
Instructors are assigned to an area to teach skills or merit badges to Scouts. 
 
 
 
Control 
Achieving and maintaining control of a group of people or particular situations can be accomplished by 
keeping these in mind: 
●​
When you lose your temper, you lose long-term status and respect. 
●​
Give people a chance to react to your directions. 
●​
Have a good reason for what you ask of a group. 
●​
Be honest in what you say and do. 
●​
Once an untruth is discovered, the repair job on your integrity is expensive. 
●​
See possible outcomes, both good and bad, before they become realities 
●​
Stay a step ahead of those you lead, but not so far ahead that they lose track of your direction. 
●​
Set the pace. 
●​
Be the role model. 
●​
Physical punishment is forbidden; for example, pushups, running laps, demeaning tasks, and 
corporal punishment are unacceptable forms of leadership. 
●​
Look for more positive ways to lead. 
●​
Be consistent and fair in discipline of Scouts. 
●​
Scouts should be referred to their Scoutmaster for discipline. 
●​
It is up to each adult leader to control the behavior of their troop members. 
●​
Be available and a friend to all. 
●​
"Cliques" or limited social groups, can produce disastrous results. 
●​
Whatever you do, wherever you go, strive to see that all are welcome. 
 
Teaching Skills 
Teaching is both an art and a skill. To be an effective teacher, you should: 
●​
Be able to perform the skill yourself. 
●​
Break the learning process into a series of steps. 
●​
Work with a small group of learners when you can, using additional instructors, if available. 
●​
Teach skills in an area free from distractions. 
●​
Size up those you are teaching both as to what they already know and their potential for learning 
more. 
●​
Set up a learning-by-doing process and don't interfere with Scouts as they try their hands at the 
skill. 
●​
On the other hand, gently correct obvious mistakes and offer encouragement if frustration appears 
to be setting in. 
●​
Never correct Scouts in a disrespectful or sarcastic manner. 
●​
Through positive remarks, encourage Scouts about their progress. 
●​
Point out the completion of each step and make special mention of steps done particularly well. 
●​
If any Scouts appear to catch on especially fast, recruit them on the spot to help others who catch 
on more slowly. 
●​
Urge Scouts to practice and perhaps even to teach others, once they have mastered the skill you 
are teaching. 
●​
Use words, pictures and other visual aids to make camp lessons come alive. 
 
 
Teaching With E.D.G.E. 
The official method Scouting America teaches everyone is the EDGE method. It’s a solid method.  
Edge stands for: 
●​
Explain: Tell them what you’re gonna tell them. 
●​
Demonstrate: Show them how to do it. 
●​
Guide: Walk them through it, letting them try it a couple times. 
●​
Enable: Test and give them the tools to go on to take the skill with them into the future. 
20 

 
  
Game Design Method 
That’s awesome and all, but consider this… 
The best video games have a very specific method to give players an enjoyable way to learn to play a game. 
It’s similar to EDGE, but a bit more fun. Let’s use Level 1-1 of the original Super Mario Bros. to illustrate.  
1.​
Explain: Oh no! Princess Toadstool has been kidnapped by the evil King Koopa! Let’s go save her! 
2.​
Demonstrate: The first thing you see in the level is a single slow moving Goomba, conveniently 
shaped like an arrow as if to imply “you should jump here.” After you’ve figured out how to target a 
jump, you’re rewarded with points, some floating bricks 
to break and some “?” block containing a power up and 
coins.  
3.​
Guide: As you go forward, you are shown a few new 
elements of the gameplay you’ll need to maneuver 
including pipes. One pipe you can go down for a bunch 
of free coins. If you choose to skip the pipe, there’s a 
hidden one up. Then you face your first first gap to jump. 
After the gap, things start ramping up. You get a second 
tier power-up (the fire flower) if you can survive the 
Goomba’s dropping down from the platform above. 
Then you meet your first Koopa Troopa that introduces a 
new game mechanic of the sliding shell. 
4.​
Enable: From there, things get more complicated and 
you will need to use all of the skills you’ve learned to 
complete the level. By the time you reach the flag, you are fully prepared to take on level 1-2.  
 
 
21 

 
So how can that possibly relate to teaching an 11 year old scout how to tie a square knot? Well try this out.  
 
1.​
Explain: Oh no! You need to tie down a parcel! Let’s tie a square knot!  
2.​
Demonstrate: You introduce the concept of a 
working end and a standing end of the rope. The 
working end is the one that does the work, the standing 
end just kind of plays along. Then show them the basic 
overhand, leading with the working end. Then another 
overhand, again leading with the working end.  
3.​
Guide: So now that they know the basics, they 
can try it out themselves. You can play around a bit with 
the terminology. Some people get the “working end vs 
standing end” method better while some people find 
the “right over left, left over right” easier to understand. 
Everyone learns a little differently so you want to 
experiment a bit to see what works for your students. 
Get them doing it and be on the move making each 
person feel comfortable. With knots, often handling the anxiety is most of the battle. It’s kinda like 
math.  
4.​
Enable: Now we actually try tying some stuff down with it. Shake things up, try out different 
variations on the premise of the square knot and challenge them. Did you know if you yank one end 
of a Granny Knot you get Two Half Hitches? Did you know you could tie a Square Knot with a bite 
around another byte? Did you know if you do that method differently, you can create a Thieves' 
Knot? The Square knot isn’t even its real name. It’s a Reef Knot, and the Ashley Book of Knots says 
it’s one of the most useless knots out there for anything actually important. Once you’ve shown 
them the amazing world of everything the Square Knot has to offer, not only are they going to walk 
away fully understanding the Square Knot, they now like knots and are primed to learn the next 
knots! 
 
I just think that’s a lot more fun than the dry “tell them what you’re going to tell them, tell them, then tell 
them what you told them” mantra in teaching.  
 
 
Communication: Darmok and Jalad at Tanagra 
 
When we are getting ready for a new summer, we have the monumental task of bringing together hardened 
veterans, green newbies, and young CITs who have never done a chore, let alone held a job (not you of 
course. You’re great!).  
 
The trick is, the veterans already know Lawton. Know how to call someone on the radio, know that when we 
say “the logs” we mean those benches over there that aren’t logs at all. We speak in a shorthand that makes 
perfect sense to us but to outsiders, it’s as baffling as an alien language.  
  
Which brings us to something very special (and very nerdy). In the classic Star Trek: The Next Generation 
episode, “Darmok,” the Enterprise has been assigned to try to establish diplomatic relations with an 
enigmatic alien race known as Tamarians. The Universal Translator is working, but giving only what seem to 
be nonsense proper nouns. Through a series of trials, Picard learns that Dathon, the Tamarian captain, 
“speaks in metaphor.” “Darmok and Jalad at Tanagra” is about two hunters who learned to understand each 
other, fought a mighty beast, and left together as friends. “Shaka when the walls fell” is failure, sadness, or 
simply “no.” 
22 

 
 
Here is how we’ll apply that lesson to survive Staff Week, connect with our campers, and keep from losing 
our minds. 
Cutting Through the Alphabet Soup 
When you rattle off camp lingo, you’re speaking Tamarian. If a senior staffer says, “Go grab the Ranger from 
the Ogre, stop by STEM in the Commissioner’s Shack and pick up their SOP and bring them to the EMT at 
the Logs,” a new staffer will stare at you like you’re loony.  
●​
Be a Cultural Guide: If you’ve been here before, it’s your responsibility to translate our traditions 
and look out for the new folks. 
●​
Explain the “Why”: Explain what you’re talking about. I’m not even sure why the Ranger’s tool shed 
is called The Black Ogre, but that’s what it’s called. The STEM program runs out of what was once 
the Commissioner’s Shack, and it still clings to that name decades after the last time a Camp 
Commissioner ever stayed in that cabin. An SOP is a Standard Operating Procedure. EMT is an 
Emergency Medical Technician, a medical certification our Medic may have. The Logs I explained 
earlier as the bleachers in front of the Dining Hall.   
●​
Reveal the Hidden Map: Camp has a "hidden curriculum"—unspoken rules about where you can 
sneak some cell signal, what time you can catch the showers when no one is around, what 
compliments work to tell the Program Director to get out of cleaning the KYBO behind the youth 
shower house (I’m kidding, that wont work… chocolate though…). Share those secrets freely. Bring 
the newbies into the cool kid club, don’t gatekeep. 
The El-Adrel Principle (The Power of Shared Toil) 
In the episode, Picard and Dathon finally bond while marooned on El-Adrel Four, fighting for survival. By 
facing a physical trial together, they bypass words and build a shared understanding.  
 
Staff Week is our El-Adrel. Shared labor is a huge equalizer and teams bond best when faced with a 
common task. Collective physical struggle builds nonverbal coordination and group trust fast. The more you 
work with different people and the harder the work is, the closer your bond will be, leading to better 
chemistry through the summer and more productivity all around.  
Assuming Positive Intent (Dealing with Friction) 
The long days of camp can be hard sometimes. You’re going to get frustrated, maybe even get angry and 
lash out. “Zinda, his face black, his eyes red” our Tamarian friends would say. 
 
When communication fails here, small gripes can turn into massive headaches. In the episode, Dathon 
throws a dagger at Picard. A "low-context" response would be to see it as an attack. Picard, however, 
realized Dathon was offering a tool for their mutual survival. 
 
You have to apply that same grace to your coworkers and campers: 
●​
Look at the Surroundings: If someone snaps at you, there’s probably a reason for it. Maybe 
they’re frustrated about something else, maybe something you did or said hit a nerve you didn’t 
know, or maybe they just have a headache. .  
●​
Truly Listen: When someone is acting out, look for the root cause rather than getting defensive. 
Talk to them privately and hear what they’re actually saying. 
●​
Speak from the “I”: Use "I statements" (like, "When this happens, I feel...") to find solutions together 
instead of starting a fight. Psychologists recommend this method for a reason. It avoids sounding 
accusatory and takes responsibility for your side of the situation. 
23 

 
Reading the Air 
Memorizing this handbook and all the safety rules won't save you when a camper is in an emotional tailspin. 
Working with youth requires empathy and the ability to sense what’s unsaid.  
●​
Identify the Cues: If a scout is hiding from their troop, they aren’t just being stubborn. They are 
likely “Darmok on the ocean,” or feeling isolated, homesick, or scared. 
●​
Lead with Heart: Use the U-S-A model: Understand why they’ve pulled away, Speak with them 
one-on-one, and Act to get them the help they need. 
 
24 

 
Campfires 
Let's get this out of the way right away. The following guidelines are passed down directly from 
National:
part_1_camp_staff_training_and_culture.campfires_and_ceremonies:
BSA Ceremonies and Campfire Guidance 
(National Camping School) 
Ceremonies and campfires are fun and exciting parts of any camping experience! They give youth the 
ability to perform, but run-ons, stories, skits and songs must also fit within the program guidelines of 
Scouting America. There are numerous things that are inappropriate and unacceptable. While the following 
is not an exhaustive list, it will help you begin the discussion about ensuring only appropriate material is 
portrayed: 
●​
No name Calling, put-downs, Hazing 
●​
No references to undergarments, nudity or bodily functios 
●​
No derogatory references to or stereotyping of ethnic or cultural backgrounds, economic 
situations, or disabilities 
●​
No portrayal of sensitive social issues such as alcohol, drugs, gangs, guns, suicide, etc. – be mindful 
of this in song lyrics as well. 
●​
Wasteful, ill-mannered, or improper use of food or water including wasting food in ANY way for 
comedic purpose is not allowed. You may not know the current situation of youth and adults who 
are in the audience. Many may not have adequate food at home and the wasting of food in any way 
would further point out this disparity. Additionally there are parts of the world where water is not 
potable therefore the wasting of water is not appropriate. 
●​
No inside jokes that exclude some of those present 
●​
Do not change lyrics to patriotic songs (“America”, “America the Beautiful”, “God Bless America”, 
“The Star-Spangled Banner”) or hymns and other spiritual songs 
●​
Do not embarrass anyone – including staff or audience members (even if they are “in on it”) - just 
because the staff member is in on it, everyone in the audience is not aware of that fact and the 
appearance is that you are making fun of someone. 
●​
Do not portray violent behavior or any behavior not in line with the Guide to Safe Scouting (ex. 
pointing “guns” at each other) 
●​
No bathroom humor or skits/songs where a toilet is the punchline or a part of the skit or song. 
●​
No water skits – NO ONE gets wet in any way (includes staff, and even if they are “in on it”) 
●​
No material with sexual overtones 
●​
Do not include anything that is not in keeping with the ideals of Scouting America.  
 
The best/ motto to have is, “If in doubt, take it out!”  
 
All material should be vetted by the Program Director. Be sure you understand all aspects of the 
performance and what will happen. For example, a unit may have the same name for a skit and have a 
different interpretation of it than you expected.  
​
—--------------------------------- 
So what does that mean? 
It basically means skits and songs performed in camp cannot contain anything that could possibly make 
anyone feel bad. Is it a bit heavy handed? Probably. That doesn’t make it any less important. The important 
thing to remember is that camp is meant to be a positive, safe space for everyone. That includes 
25 

 
everyone from disabled or special needs people to people who hate the word “moist.” They all deserve to 
have a good experience without feeling mocked.  
 
This probably knocks out a lot of the skits and songs you already know.  
 
But that's ok! Creativity thrives in adversity. Most of the skits and songs you know are probably over 50 
years old anyway! Let's make up some new stuff! 
 
Making campers laugh is one of the best parts of working on staff, but it takes a little finesse to pull off a 
campfire that is genuinely funny, memorable, and appropriate. Here is an advanced guide for writing and 
performing solid comedy, pulling the best advice from professional writers and performers. 
 
Performance Fundamentals 
Before we get too deep, here are the basic natural laws of performing in a campfire. 
●​
E-nun-ci-ate - You can’t tell a joke if the audience can’t hear or understand you. Speak clearly, 
deliberately, and a little bit louder than you think feels necessary.  
●​
Pacing - Even the funniest joke gets boring if it overstays its welcome. Pacing is more an art than a 
science. This is why rehearsal is extremely helpful.  
●​
Rule of 3 - It’s one of the oldest rules on comedy for a reason. One beat to establish the scene, one 
beat to set up the joke, and one beat for the punchline.  
1.​
A duck walks up to a lemonade stand and asks for grapes, gets a no, and waddles away.  
2.​
The duck walks up again and the angry clerk threatens to nail his bill to the floor if he asks 
again.  
3.​
The duck walks up again and asks for nails. Upon getting a confused “no”, he asks for 
grapes.  
There are ways to get more mileage out of a joke if you and the audience are really enjoying it, but 
in those cases there needs to be a proper story arc so that you’re not just repeating the same gag 
ad nauseum. Again, rehearse.  
●​
Read the audience - You’re not up there in a vacuum. You’ve got an audience you’re trying to 
entertain. Take a moment to pay attention to them. How are they feeling? Is your song too long? Are 
your skits too rushed? Is it cold out? Are there bugs making them miserable? All of these things 
should be accounted for.  
●​
Don’t sweat the mistakes - The biggest laughs you’re likely to get are bound to be when you screw 
something up. That’s ok. Embrace it. Make it part of the skit. Carefully following a script will lead to 
madness. More complicated skits often need a script to hit all the right beats, but good reliable skits 
are built off of improvisation and personality.  
●​
Enthusiasm, energy, absurdity - The fact of the matter is, if you leave that campfire stage with any 
dignity left, you did it wrong.  
Leading Songs 
How do you start a song?  
Do you say, “who wants to sing Tootie Ta?” NO!!! Do you ask “what do you want to sing?” They’ll answer with 
everything from “Nothing!” to the latest wildly inappropriate pop song.  
 
The best way to start a song is to START DOING IT! Just run up to the crowd and yell as loud as you can, 
“THIS IS A REPEAT AFTER ME SONG!” Repeat songs are the easiest way to get started because they pretty 
much force participation. After you’ve got them going, you can go with the more casual singalongs. 
Enthusiasm is everything. If you’re having a blast out there, they’ll follow.  
 
But why do we even sing songs? No one really likes it! 
They say they don’t. I don’t always. Just let me sit and suffer through my morning in peace and quiet until I 
can eat my food. It’s a fair feeling. The reason we do it is because songs bring people together. It’s a 
psychological thing. When you’re out there sticking out your buns during Tootie-Ta, you’re part of 
something. Something silly, but it means something very real deep down. There’s a reason churches (and, 
26 

 
admittedly, cults) sing hymns and repeat ritualistic phrases. It opens them up to the communal spirit and 
makes them more open to embracing what we have to offer them. Scouts who spent a week singing songs 
and yelling in unison will leave camp feeling like they had a much more powerful experience than those who 
just sat on the logs waiting for their food. What makes us not a cult, exactly? Well, we usually serve generic 
brand lemonade, not Kool-aid.  
 
 
The Campfire Arc  
A campfire should have an arc not entirely unlike the fire itself. It should start off with a bang, something 
active, exciting, rousing. Then put some of your best stuff up front. You can fit in the more mundane stuff in 
the middle, then "throw another log on the fire" with more of your best stuff as the climax. Then one or two 
slower but still memorable bits (a meaningful song or story) before your inspirational ending.  
 
 
In the case of a Staff Campfire, don’t MC it. The staff should know what they’re doing, you don’t need to 
introduce the next skit. Saturday Night Live doesn’t say “up next, the game show parody and on deck is 
Weekend Update.” Just keep the surprises coming.  
 
When it comes to the Camper Campfire, we usually have one or two SPL’s do the job, and they’ll need to 
introduce the next troop and who’s on deck. Don’t say the name of the skit, just in case the name gives 
away the punchline. Just the troop. Remember, the Program Director MUST approve all campfire content.  
 
Part 3 is a “Campfire Master Class and Songbook” for you to take your comedy skills to a 
whole new level. For now, however, we’ve got to shift the tone a bit and dig in on some of the 
crucial policies and procedures necessary in running a summer camp. 
 
27 

 
Part 2 
Policies, 
Procedures, 
Guidelines, and 
Laws 
 
28 

 
 
And now we come to it. The training material so far has just been “good to know,” but now we enter the 
critical content. It’s no joke, lives depend on this stuff.  
 
We start with weather safety. We are spending the summer in the mountains, deep in the forest with one 
paved road out of here (and a back road I'd be scared to take even in an atv). We get lightning, rain, hail, 
flooding, cold weather, and hot weather. It all matters very much to how you survive and how you’ll help our 
scouts survive.  
 
Note that Scouting America requires all adult leaders and staff to complete the Hazardous Weather training 
module online every year. The content below is just a cheat sheet in case you need to reference it while at 
camp.

part_2_policies_procedures_guidelines_an.safeguarding_youth:
Safeguarding Youth guidelines apply, more on that in Part 2, but otherwise there should be no 
difference between them and Junior Staff, besides hopefully a bit more maturity and leadership skill. Adult 
staff often consider themselves exempt from staff activities like running logs, campfires, and meal and 
shower cleanup. That is not the case. However, age brings with it some privileges and some limitations. 
Adults are more likely to be allowed to opt out of activities, be it a strong dislike of the activity or their age 
interferes with their involvement. As soon as you hit 30, parts of your body tend to start sabotaging you, 
and it gets worse every year. Running around leading 20 minutes of rousing camp songs is not always in the 
cards. That said, it is not fair or appropriate to ask the youth staff to do work the adults won't do. That of 
course comes with the caveat that the administration, medic, kitchen staff, ranger, Trading Post, 
commissioner, and area directors likely have their own tasks that take priority. Coffee is not necessarily one 
of them.  
 
Support Staff 
While they might not spend their day on "center stage" teaching merit badges, many essential departments 
keep the camp running behind the scenes. This includes the kitchen, maintenance, Commissioners, 
Health Lodge, Trading Post, and Headquarters. Make no mistake: these departments are every bit as 
vital as the program areas. Without these dedicated staff members, Lawton simply would not function. 
These support services follow the same leadership structure, each led by an Area Director and supported 
by their own assistants. 
Support staff work incredibly hard, often in high-pressure situations, to ensure the needs of every Scout and 
fellow staffer are met. They deserve the exact same level of respect and courtesy as the program team. We 
are one family, and every single person is a critical piece of the puzzle.
part_2_policies_procedures_guidelines_an.severe_weather_preparedness:
Severe Weather Preparedness 
Training Requirements & Compliance 
●​
Mandatory for Staff: Under National Camp Accreditation Program (NCAP) Standard SQ-402, all 
camp staff must complete the Hazardous Weather Training online before youth 
participants arrive. It is also required for all registered Direct Contact Leaders (e.g., Scoutmasters, 
Cubmasters). 
●​
Two-Year Renewal: The certification is valid for exactly two years (730 days) and must be kept 
current. 
●​
Online Only: The training takes about 30 minutes aynd must be completed individually online 
via My.Scouting.org. In-person or group-facilitated classes are strictly prohibited and will not be 
recorded in official training systems. 
Lightning Safety & The 30/30 Rule 
●​
"When Thunder Roars, Go Indoors": If you can hear thunder, lightning is a danger. 
●​
The 30-Second Rule: If the time between a lightning flash and the thunder is 30 seconds or less, 
the storm is within 6 miles. Suspend all outdoor activities immediately and seek shelter. 
●​
The 30-Minute Rule: Do not leave shelter or resume outdoor activities until at least 30 minutes 
have passed since the last audible thunder or visible lightning. 
●​
Safe Shelters: You must use a primary shelter (an enclosed building with a roof, walls, floor, and 
plumbing/wiring) or a secondary shelter (a fully enclosed hard-topped metal vehicle with rolled-up 
windows). In our case, the primary lightning and storm shelter is the Dining Hall.  
●​
Tents, open pavilions, and golf carts offer zero protection from lightning.  
●​
Caught in the Open: If you cannot reach shelter, avoid tall/isolated trees, water, and metal objects. 
Disperse the group so individuals are 15 to 100 feet apart, and do NOT lie flat on the ground. 
Heat & Thermal Stress Mitigation 
●​
Acclimation & Hydration: we are at almost 8,000 ft above sea level and more than 5,000 ft above 
Tucson. It takes a few days to properly adjust to the decreased air pressure. Be ready for that and 
prepare scouts for it. The larger issue at Camp Lawton is dehydration. The environment will 
dehydrate you quickly. You and the scouts should be drinking several liters of water a day. Don't 
force it, over-hydration is a thing too, but drink when you're thirsty. Alternate it with some 
electrolyte sports drink if you're having any trouble. If you're feeling a headache, nose bleed, dizzy, 
and/or fatigued, sit down and relax in the shade for a few minutes sipping your water. If it continues, 
get to the Health Lodge. Instruct scouts (and leaders) to do the same.  
●​
Heat Exhaustion vs. Heatstroke: 
29 

 
○​
Heat Exhaustion (pale/clammy skin, heavy sweating, nausea): Move to shade, remove 
excess clothing, cool with cold water, and hydrate. Do not give salt tablets. 
○​
Heatstroke (altered mental state, confusion, extremely hot/flushed skin): This is a 
life-threatening emergency. Call EMS/911 immediately, initiate rapid cooling (ice packs, 
cold water immersion), and do not give anything by mouth. 
○​
Camp Lawton is in the mountains. It rarely gets much higher than 90°, nothing like what 
we're used to in the desert, but it's still enough to get sick from the heat. Hypothermia is 
also not impossible in our environment.  
High Winds & Hazard Trees 
●​
Activity Suspension: High winds or microbursts can cause extreme localized damage. Climbing 
must be suspended immediately if winds exceed predetermined camp limits. We also may shut 
down Range and Target Activities if the range master determines the wind is unsafe. 
●​
Hazard Trees ("Widowmakers"): Look out for dead trees, live trees with dead parts, or unstable 
structures. Never set up tents, hammocks, or program areas within a hazard tree's fall 
radius. The fall radius on flat ground is 1.5 times the total height of the tree. Management works 
with the Forest Service each year to bring down hazard trees, but thanks to fires, bark beetles, 
droughts, and storms, there are bound to still be some trees that may pose a threat. Be aware of 
these, stay clear, and watch for any leaning trees, signs of the trees swaying more than usual in the 
wind and listen for cracking sounds. Report these to the Camp Director. 
Camp Emergency Action Plans (EAP) & Alarms 
●​
Property-Specific EAP: Every camp has a specific, written Emergency Action Plan covering 
procedures for weather, fires, lost campers, and medical emergencies. All staff must be trained on 
this and have access to it. 
●​
Camp Lawton’s EAP: In the event of an emergency, the camp bell will begin ringing continuously. 
EVERYONE must report directly to the parade grounds (the flags). There we'll do a head count, 
and proceed with further instructions. We'll give some more detail on this later.  
Safeguarding Youth 
Scouting is still recovering from the fallout of some very dark stories. It’s uncomfortable to talk about, but it 
must be addressed. The important thing to understand is that it’s not about the individual abusers here; it’s 
about the culture they create that allows the abuse. A scoutmaster doesn’t crawl into the sleeping bag of a 
youth if there are people around that are unwilling to allow it. Our commitment to Safeguarding Youth is not 
just about preventing abuse. It’s about making Camp Lawton a place where that could never happen.   
The following is a quick reference for the Safeguarding Youth Training content. We will be addressing it 
during staff training and you will need to complete the online course module annually. These are the most 
critical rules in the scouting program, and do not allow for any grey area.  
It should also be noted that some of the guidelines have changed since the Youth Protection days, so when 
making decisions, make sure to check that the policy you're remembering is SYT not YPT.  
Safeguarding Youth Training (SYT) Requirements 
●​
Annual Requirement: All staff members and all registered adult scout leaders (18+) must complete 
the Safeguarding Youth Training annually. 
●​
Course Structure: The initial course takes 90 minutes. Subsequent annual renewals require a 
15–20 minute refresher video and an exam, which must be passed with a score of 80% or higher. 
●​
Age Transitions: Camp staff members who turn 18 during the season instantly become subject to 
all adult rules, including separate housing and the prohibition of one-on-one contact with youth. 
Supervision and Contact 
30 

 
●​
Two-Deep Leadership: A minimum of two registered adult leaders (21+) is required at all times for 
all activities. If female youth are present, at least one of the leaders must be a registered female 
adult. 
●​
No One-on-One Contact: One-on-one contact between adults (including staff 18+) and youth is 
strictly prohibited in person, online, via text, or on any digital platform. 
●​
The Buddy System: Youth must always use the buddy system. Buddy pairs must be single-gender 
and no more than two years apart in age. Staff members or adults (18+) cannot be buddies with 
youth members. 
Privacy and Accommodations 
●​
Separate Accommodations: Male and female youth, as well as youth and adults, must have 
completely separate tenting and lodging. No adult or staff member over 18 may enter or sleep 
in youth lodging. 
●​
Restroom Privacy: Separate shower and latrine facilities must be provided for adults and youth, 
and for males and females. Camp Lawton has designated adult and youth shower houses, and each 
has individual private facilities. Staff Hill also has individual private facilities with locks for showers 
and toilets.   
●​
Respect of Privacy: Adults may only intrude on youth privacy for health and safety emergencies. 
The use of cameras, smartphones, mirrors, or drones in areas where privacy is expected (like 
showers or changing areas) is strictly prohibited. 
Behavior and Discipline 
●​
Constructive Discipline: Discipline must always be constructive, positive, and reflect Scouting's 
values. Corporal punishment, isolation, humiliation, and ridicule are strictly prohibited. 
●​
Zero Tolerance: There is a strict zero-tolerance policy for hazing, initiations, secret organizations, 
bullying, cyberbullying, or physical violence. 
●​
No Sexual Activity: All sexual activity and inappropriate public displays of affection are strictly 
prohibited at camp. 
●​
Appropriate Attire: Modest and proper clothing is required at all times. For example, swimwear 
must be modest (e.g., no tight-fitting swim briefs or bikinis). 
Incident Response and Mandatory Reporting 
●​
Intervene Immediately: If you witness abuse, policy violations, or unsafe behavior, you must step 
in immediately to stop it. Do not dismiss inappropriate actions as "just kids being kids". 
●​
Confidentiality: Any information concerning campers is strictly confidential. This is of the utmost 
importance when dealing with suspected cases of child abuse. The camper must never be 
informed of this notification process. His/her knowledge of this report could endanger him/her 
when he/she returns home.  
●​
The Perpetrator: If an alleged perpetrator is on camp grounds, they will be immediately separated 
from the campers until the incident is resolved, until the threat is removed, or as long as necessary 
to protect the safety and welfare of the campers.  
●​
Mandatory Reporters: All camp staff and volunteers are mandated reporters. This duty cannot be 
delegated to anyone else. 
●​
Reporting Chain: You must immediately report any good-faith suspicion of abuse or policy 
violations to the Camp Director. At that point, both of you are responsible for contacting the 
necessary authorities. You will need to speak to DCS as described below while the Camp Director 
contacts the Scout Executive. The reason for this painful process is to prevent any chance or 
accusations of institutional concealment by the organization. Don't be afraid of this, you're doing 
the right thing. 
●​
Scouts First Helpline: Scouting America offers the 24-hour Scouts First Helpline at 
1-844-SCOUTS1 (1-844-726-8871) in the event you need immediate assistance regarding a youth 
safety concern and the Camp Director is unavailable.  
●​
Immediate Risk: Call 911 immediately if someone is at immediate risk of harm. 
31 

 
The Law: Mandatory Reporting (ARS 13-3620) As a camp staff member, YOU are a mandated reporter. 
This isn't just a Scouting policy; it is Arizona State Law (ARS 13-3620). YOU (not the Camp Director or 
Council Executive) are legally required to report all concerns of child abuse or neglect to The Arizona 
Department of Child Safety (DCS). Reach out to the 24/7 state hotline by calling 1-888-SOS-CHILD 
(888-767-2445). In a life-threatening crisis, skip the hotline and dial 911 immediately. While mandated 
reporters can submit non-emergency details via the Guardian online portal, the simple reality is that every 
citizen is legally required to report suspected abuse without delay to the Department of Child Safety or 
the local authorities. 
Legal Definitions you need to know: 
1.​
Child Abuse: Harm or threatened harm to a child's health or welfare, which occurs through 
non-accidental physical or mental injury, sexual abuse, or maltreatment. 
2.​
Child Neglect: Harm to a child's health or welfare by a person responsible for them, which occurs 
through negligent treatment (failure to provide adequate food, clothing, shelter, emotional, or 
medical care). 
 
32 

 
Policies and Procedures
part_2_policies_procedures_guidelines_an.the_camp_lawton_guidelines:
The Camp Lawton Guidelines (What we expect from everyone in camp)  
As staff, it is your job to help gently but firmly enforce the rules of the camp. Every camper and leader 
agrees to the following standards when they arrive, and they naturally apply to you too: 
1.​
The Rule of Two: The buddy system is required at all times. Specifically, buddies must be same-sex 
and within two years difference.  
2.​
Boundaries: Camp staff housing and the kitchen are strictly off-limits to campers. Only the 
residents of a cabin may enter a cabin, including adults.  
3.​
Respect the Facilities: Litter, vandalism, and reckless behavior is unacceptable. Water is limited in 
camp. Don’t waste it.  
4.​ Safety: Closed-toed shoes must be worn at all times outside.  
5.​
Zero Tolerance for Put-Downs: Swearing, negative talk, and bullying are strictly forbidden.  
6.​
No Pranks: Even the best carried out pranks can escalate quickly, cause hurt feelings, and cause 
property damage and hurt feelings.  They are not permitted. The occasional “practical joke” can be 
funny, but must be approved by the camp director.  
7.​
The Camper's Covenant: Every participant agrees to the following: "I will not bring any type of 
weapon(s) or use any tobacco products, alcohol or drugs, except for prescribed medical purposes. 
I will behave as a respectful person at all times." 
All Areas  
All areas are expected to keep gear stored and locked in a secure place whenever it is unsupervised. Areas 
must remain clean, and trash must be taken to the trash area behind the kitchen at the end of each day. 
Areas will be asked to work on projects throughout the summer to improve their areas aesthetically.  
 
At least two staff members must be in the area any time Scouts are present (as per Youth Protection 
Guidelines). In the event of an emergency in the area, attempt to use the camp radios to call for the Medic 
and Camp Director. If the radios are not working for any reason, a staff member should be dispatched to 
deliver the message by foot. 
 
Radios 
Camp Lawton has a limited number of high quality radios capable of reaching anywhere in camp (under the 
right conditions).  
●​
These are for official purposes only as they are subject to FCC regulations. 
●​
When using the radios, press the button – Wait a second – Begin your call. 
●​
Calls should be “[YOU] to [AREA YOU’RE CALLING]”.  
●​
The response should be “[YOUR AREA], go ahead.” Specific wording can vary, but keep it in the 
ballpark.  
●​
Do not use real names over the radio. Use program areas or code names established in advance by 
the administration. 
●​
Be straight to the point and get off. Don’t tie up the radios. 
●​
Remember that it’s not only fellow staff that may be hearing your calls.  
 
The Kitchen 
The kitchen is off limits unless explicitly permitted by the Cook or Camp Director. This is to ensure health 
and safety for everyone.  
 
Special Diets 
For anyone with special diets, be it medical or ideology, the kitchen will be happy to accommodate to their 
best (reasonable) ability. For the best experience, please inform leadership and the kitchen as soon as 
possible before camp.  
 
33 

 
Pets 
Personal pets are not permitted in camp. The Forest Service prohibits animals without special permitting 
due to the potential to draw dangerous situations with the local wildlife. They can also be a distraction in 
camp. Small mammals, small birds, and non-venomous reptiles may be temporarily donated to the Nature 
Lodge, but during the summer they become an educational tool of the Nature Lodge, maintained by the 
Nature staff, not pets. If you plan to donate an animal, discuss it in advance with the Nature Director who 
will then clear it with the Camp Director. If cleared to do so, you must provide all materials needed for taking 
care of the animal. The camp will purchase refills of food and bedding as needed.  
 
Laundry 
Clean clothes are an important part of how you present yourself and represent the camp. However, we do 
not have a washing machine in camp. Have multiple uniforms, plenty of socks, underwear, and shirts. If 
necessary, manually washing clothes the old fashioned way may be your best option. If absolutely 
necessary, we can try to arrange a run down the mountain to a laundramat.    
 
Staff Use of Camp Equipment 
Camp materials are always available for use as needed, but you must be thrifty. Take excellent care of all 
equipment and return it to where it belongs. If you use a finite resource, make sure you don’t impact the 
inventory in a way that will impair the program or the camper experience.  
 
Scout Advancement for Staff 
Youth staff members are encouraged to work on their own advancement while at camp. With the approval 
of your home Scoutmaster, the Program Director or Camp Director may sign off on merit badges and rank 
advancement. However, advancement should not be your primary goal at camp. Think of it more as a 
convenient perk.  
  
Payment of Salaries  
For legal and council policy purposes, paid staff must have an IRS W-4 form, an I-9 form, a completed Staff 
Application and a Social Security Number filed and the appropriate deductions made. The value of room 
and board will be added to and deducted from each check in keeping with IRS policies. To be clear, that’s 
just a lot of handwaving for the benefit of the feds. It cancels out, you're not actually being charged for your 
room and board.  
 
Tips and Gratuities 
It is not consistent with Scouting to expect or receive tips, gratuities, or gifts from unit leaders or scouts. 
Offers should be declined.  
 
Leaving Camp 
Staff members may only leave camp with the prior permission of the Camp Director. For any camp 
sponsored activity that will take place off the camp property, there must be a sign-up list, posted in the 
Camp Office. This list will be used as a roster for the outing as well as the group sign out. Before leaving 
camp, take a roll to double check that the list is consistent with who is going on the outing. Make a copy of 
the final list and post it with the sign out board at the Camp Office. Do head counts regularly and sign back 
in when returning. If a Scout decides to return to camp early, it must be reported back at camp. 
 
Sign-in / Sign Out 
A clip board should be posted at the camp office where anyone entering or leaving camp must sign in and 
out. Visitors must be approved by the Camp Director before arrival and must sign in at the Guest Sign In 
sheet at the office IMMEDIATELY upon arrival. This is a national safety and security rule and can not be 
negotiated. 
 
Visitors 
Be sure that visitors give you advance notice of their plans to come to camp. They must be approved by the 
Program or Camp Director before arrival and must sign in at the Guest Sign-In sheet at the Camp OƯice 
34 

 
immediately upon arrival. Guests must also sign out when leaving the camp. Visitors are expected to follow
