all camp rules and dress appropriately. 
Visitors that have permission from the Camp Director to spend the night at camp, must have a completed 
BSA Health Form, Parts A & B and be checked by the Health Officer. 
 
Staff Vehicles and Transportation 
Staff may park on Staff Hill. All vehicles must be backed in and faced toward the exit road for quick 
departure in case of an emergency. There is limited space, so carpool where possible. No motorized 
vehicles (including ATVs, golf carts, and scooters) are permitted within the camp proper unless vetted and 
explicitly cleared by the Camp Director. All staff under the age of 18 must have written parental approval 
and the approval of the Camp Director to have a motor vehicle on camp premises. Transportation to and 
from camp is the responsibility of the individual staff member. Junior staƯ being transported by persons 
other than their parents must have written parental permission. StaƯ vehicles must always remain in the 
parking lot. Only approved vehicles may be within the camp property. 
 
Emergency Leave 
Stuff happens. We don't expect this job to take priority over your real life. Whether it's a death in the family 
or a close friend’s bar mitzvah, you should be there for your people. To request leave, speak to the Camp 
Director with as much notice as possible and we'll make it work.  
 
Health Examination 
As with all long term Scouting America outings, you are required to have medical forms A, B, and C on file in 
camp. Part A is a release form, B is medications and medical history, and C is an annual evaluation by a 
medical professional. Your presence in camp overnight at all, let alone your job, is contingent on completing 
these forms before camp. 
 
Worker's Compensation 
As is standard practice by any employer, you are entitled to worker's comp coverage, meaning Catalina 
Council will pay your medical bills if you are injured while doing your job in camp. This is a lot of paperwork 
— I mean, we care deeply about all of our staff, so we'd prefer you don't have to use this benefit, but if you 
do, we got you.  
 
Alcohol, Tobacco, Pornography and Drugs 
The consuming or possession of alcoholic beverages is strictly forbidden on Camp Lawton property, even 
for those over 21. Underaged possession of tobacco and ‘vape’ equipment is similarly forbidden. Tobacco 
products possessed by persons 21 or older must be kept secure in a locked vehicle. Cigarettes may only be 
smoked inside of your car with the window up. Vapes only while no youth is present. Limited use of 
Marajuana is legal for recreational purposes in Arizona, but not in Camp Lawton. No possession or use of 
any form of Marajuana is permitted anywhere in Camp Lawton. The same goes for being under the 
influence of marajuana, alcohol, or illicit drugs. Violation of any of these rules, as well as the possession, use 
or sale of illegal drugs or prescription pharmaceuticals not prescribed to you, will lead to immediate 
termination. Possession of physical, video, or online pornography is prohibited on camp grounds. 
Navigation to one of these sites over the camp-provided wifi will automatically be escalated directly to the 
Catalina Council Chief Scout Executive.  
 
Job Performance and Appraisals 
Area Directors will be asked to do evaluations on each staff member. These are meant simply as feedback, 
not as a disciplinary process. A bad evaluation should be thought of as a useful tool allowing you to know 
exactly where to improve and become the staff member we knew you could be when we hired you. We 
want you to excel. Area Directors will also be evaluated by the Program Director, and the Program Director 
is evaluated by the Camp Director. The Camp Director is evaluated too, by the Council Executive.   
 
Staff Quarters 
Maintaining an orderly and hygienic living environment is a core expectation. Because you may share a 
cabin with up to seven other colleagues, mutual respect for personal space is paramount. Keep pathways 
35 

 
clear of obstructions by arranging your equipment thoughtfully. All belongings should be tucked away 
neatly, and we ask that you keep your bunk looking sharp. To keep your gear secure and organized, we 
recommend using one or two lockable trunks that fit under or beside your bed. Don't forget to bring a 
laundry bag—nobody wants your dirty socks becoming a shared sensory experience! 
While we respect your privacy, the administration may conduct inspections if we have concerns regarding 
health, safety, or prohibited items. We reserve the right to visit cabins at reasonable times, always adhering 
strictly to Safeguarding Youth protocols. Occasionally, we may even show our facilities to visitors. While 
we’ll try to give you a heads-up, remember that your quarters are essentially an extension of the "onstage" 
experience. Don't let your mess break the magic! 
 
Religious Services 
The Scouting movement recognizes religion as an integral part of the character-building process and 
encourages staff to set an example for campers by openly participating in their own religious practices. The 
camp offers a camp-wide non-denominational chapel service every Sunday. All staff are invited and 
encouraged to attend this service. The chapel area is open to all personal and public faith practices at 
nearly any time, but in the case of an emergency you should make sure someone knows you're down there. 
Trading Post 
The Trading Post is the camp's primary income source besides camp fees. During approved time off, staff 
are welcome to enjoy the Trading Post as much as you like. Staff discounts, free items, and “tabs” will not be 
available. All merchandise must be paid for in cash or by card at the time of purchase. 
 
Recreational Items 
Recreational items like smartphones, books, media devices, playing cards, role playing games, and video 
games must be kept in your quarters and may be used only when that use will not interfere with your duties. 
You are responsible to see that the camp is not disturbed by sound from their entertainment devices. Music 
may not be played outside of the cabin, including on the porch Lights Out is to be honored so that everyone 
can receive proper rest. Music in work areas is restricted to the Kitchen and Trading Post, or for special 
programs approved by the Program Director. Headphones are a good option when listening to your own 
music in your quarters but they are not allowed off of Staff Hill.  
 
Media content must be Scout-appropriate, not containing any sexual activity, excessive violence, and 
strong language.  
 
You bring these devices and items to camp at your own risk as there is always a chance of them being 
damaged or stolen. Catalina Council assumes no liability for any damaged or stolen personal gear. 
 
Recreational items are privileged items. Permission to have them at camp may be revoked if abused.  
 
 
36
part_2_policies_procedures_guidelines_an.health_and_safety:
HEALTH AND SAFETY 
 
Emergencies And Other Incidents 
injury 
The safety of our campers and the staff is paramount. Everyone is a Safety Officer. If you see an unsafe act, 
stop the activity. Report all potential safety hazards to the senior staff. 
 
Injuries  
Immediately report injuries, no matter how minor, to the nearest Area Director. Every program area contains 
a first aid kit, and minor injuries may be treated on-site, but must be reported for documentation. If unsure 
of the extent of the injury or the party seems unable to walk, send for first aid through a staff member or 
radio and do not move the victim. If a back or collar-bone injury is suspected, no matter how hard they fell, 
DO NOT MOVE the victim until the trained and Medical Officer has evaluated the injury. 
 
 
Intruders  
Any unauthorized persons in camp should be reported immediately to the Camp Director. All visitors must 
check in and out with the Camp Director, and must sign in and out on the sheet at the Health Lodge 
 
Missing Person Protocol 
When a missing person is reported to any staff member, it immediately becomes the number one priority 
for the entire camp until the "all clear" is given. 
1.​ Gather Details - The first staff member aware of the situation must quickly obtain an accurate 
profile of the missing individual. Crucial details include:  
a.​ First name 
b.​ Unit number 
c.​ Physical description (especially what they are wearing) 
d.​ Last known location 
2.​ Initiate Code Blue - Strict radio protocol is vital during this phase. Immediately report the situation 
over the radio as a "Code Blue" directly to the Camp Director, relaying all the gathered details. 
3.​ Search Coordination - While all staff must immediately stand by and prepare to respond, the 
actual physical search will be organized and directed exclusively by the Camp Director or a 
designated director. Centralizing the command ensures the search is systematic, safe, and 
thorough without overlooking obvious clues. 
4.​ At the Camp Director’s discretion, the Emergency Bell may be rung to get a complete camp head 
count.  
 
Emergency Alarm Procedures 
The camp-wide alarm is the large dining hall bell. If it rings continuously, it signals an immediate emergency 
or drill. While we will attempt to communicate over the radios, be prepared to rely entirely on direct, 
face-to-face communication in the event of a power outage or radio failure. 
1.​ Secure Hazards and Evacuate - The moment you hear the continuous bell, drop whatever you are 
doing. The only exception is to quickly secure any immediate hazards in your program area, such as 
extinguishing campfires or securing dangerous equipment. If you do need to stay behind to secure 
the area, make sure to send word to the Camp Director of your situation so you are accounted for.  
Once your area is safe, immediately escort any scouts with you directly to the Parade Grounds. Do 
37 

 
not allow scouts to wander back to their campsites to look for their troop; everyone must route 
directly to the main assembly point.  
2.​ Assembly and Accountability - At the Parade Grounds, scouts will reunite with their troops and 
line up by cabin. A strict headcount will be taken directly from the official camp roster records. If 
anyone is unaccounted for, centralized search efforts will be launched from this location.  
3.​ Briefing and Next Steps - Once roll is complete and the camp is accounted for, staff will be pulled 
off to the side to receive specific operational instructions, while the Scouts and adult leaders are 
given their respective guidance.  
4.​ Weekly Drills - An emergency drill will be conducted within the first 24 hours of every camp week. 
Real emergencies happen, so treat these drills with absolute seriousness to ensure rapid, 
muscle-memory responses from both staff and campers. 
 
Bear and Wildlife Safety 
Camp Lawton is located within active black bear habitat. While sightings are rare, it does not take much for 
a wildlife encounter to escalate. Strict adherence to camp policies is our most effective method for 
preventing these incidents.  
1.​ Prevention: The "Smellables" Policy - No food, drink, or "smellables" (toiletries, deodorant, and 
other scented products) are allowed anywhere in camp except in the immediate vicinity of the 
Dining Hall. The only exception is plain water. 
a.​ Storage: Overnight, all smellables must be secured in the "Bear Box" and brought to the 
Smellables Shed located next to the youth shower house. 
b.​ Enforcement: If you see anyone with food or scented items outside of allowed areas, 
politely instruct them to relocate the items to the proper storage area, and report the 
incident to senior staff. 
2.​ Protocol for Bear Sightings - If you see a bear in camp, remain calm. Black bears will usually 
ignore you or run off if you make your presence known and show yourself to be a human threat. 
a.​ Immediately report the sighting to the Camp Director as a Code Brown. 
b.​ If it is safe to do so, adult staff should maintain a distant, safe visual on the animal so camp 
administration can track its movements. 
c.​ The Camp Director will handle contacting the Game and Fish Department. 
3.​ In the Event of an Attack - In the highly unlikely event of an attack, the immediate priority is 
scaring the animal away from the victim.  
a.​ Scare the Bear: Yell loudly and throw rocks, but do not approach the bear.  
b.​ Call for Help: Immediately dispatch someone to locate the Camp Director and the Ranger. 
c.​ First Aid & Assembly: As soon as the animal retreats, begin treating the victim with first 
aid. The emergency alarm (continuous bell) will be sounded. All scouts and staff who are 
not actively treating the victim must report immediately to the main assembly area. 
 
Other Potentially Dangerous Critters 
Also regulars at Camp Lawton are turkeys, rattlesnakes, black widow spiders, and mountain lions (only seen 
during the winter, but they have been seen). As a general rule, just stay back and observe from a safe 
distance. If you believe there is any risk at all with the animal, report it to the Camp Director and follow the 
bear sighting protocols.  
 
Fire Safety and Evacuation Procedures 
Wildfires are a reality in this environment, making strict adherence to fire safety and emergency 
communication protocols essential. 
38 

 
1.​ Campfire Regulations - All campfires are subject to current Forest Service regulations. When fires 
are permitted, they must never be built above knee level (2 to 3 feet high) and must be monitored 
closely. 
2.​ Fire in Camp (Immediate Threat) - If you observe a wildfire or an out-of-control campfire within 
the camp, immediately contact a member of the camp administration or senior staff. The camp 
alarm will sound so the threat can be evaluated and addressed.  
3.​ Distant Fires (Monitoring Phase) - If there is a wildfire on the mountain that does not pose an 
immediate threat to the camp, your job is to remain calm and keep the Scouts relaxed.Do not 
speculate or spread rumors about what you think is happening. Information will be distributed 
through official channels only. Spreading rumors during an emergency creates unnecessary panic 
and will result in disciplinary action. 
4.​ Evacuation Protocol - If an evacuation becomes necessary, the camp-wide alarm will sound, and 
everyone will assemble for specific instructions. The absolute first priority for all staff is assisting 
the Scouts and ensuring they evacuate safely. Retrieving personal gear is a secondary concern and 
will only be permitted if leadership deems it completely safe to do so. 
 
Lightning and Severe Thunderstorms 
1.​ The 30-Second Rule (Danger Threshold) - Any sign of lightning followed by thunder within less 
than 30 seconds is considered an immediate danger. When this threshold is reached, all outdoor 
activities must cease immediately.  
2.​ Seeking Shelter 
a.​ Primary Action: If you can do so safely, all staff and campers should relocate to the Dining 
Hall and remain there until the "all clear" is given. 
b.​ Shelter in Place: If you are already in a safe, secure area and it is unsafe to travel across 
camp, stay where you are and immediately radio the Camp Director with your location. 
3.​ Staff Assignments and Site Checks - During a storm, camp administration may need to dispatch 
staff to check on specific Troop campsites.  
a.​ Do Not Self-Assign: Never take on this responsibility without direct orders from a 
supervisor. Ensure your exact whereabouts are known by leadership at all times. 
b.​ Movement Hazards: If you are assigned to move through camp to check sites, strictly 
avoid poles, large trees, and open high ground. 
4.​ Escalation and Evacuation - If lightning strikes dangerously close and an immediate threat to the 
campsites is assumed, the camp alarm (continuous bell) will sound to trigger a mandatory 
evacuation of those areas to the Dining Hall. Prior to an alarm sounding, it remains the individual 
troops' prerogative to seek heavier shelter if they feel their campsite is inadequate. 
 
Fatality Protocol 
In the tragic event of a fatality at camp, immediate scene preservation, respect for the deceased, and 
limiting camper exposure are the absolute priorities. 
1.​ Immediate Reporting - Notify the first available director or senior staff member immediately. They 
will take charge of the scene and instantly contact the Camp Director and the Health Officer.  
2.​ Secure and Shield the Area 
a.​ Clear Onlookers: Immediately remove all campers, adult leaders, and unnecessary staff 
from the vicinity. 
b.​ Cover the Individual: Staff should respectfully cover the deceased to ensure privacy and 
minimize trauma to bystanders. 
39 

 
3.​ Preserve the Scene - Touch nothing and move nothing. Until authorities arrive, the area must be 
strictly preserved. Do not disturb the individual, any equipment, personal items, or the surrounding 
environment. 
4.​ Leadership Handover and Support - Once senior staff and the Camp Director arrive, they will 
take exclusive control of the situation, the scene, and all official communications. Because an 
incident of this nature is highly traumatic, professional counseling and support services will be 
provided to campers and staff as needed. 
 
Unexpected Incidences  
The camp has experienced everything from floods to Gila Monsters in camp. For reasons such as these it is 
understandable that not all incidents have a written plan. In these situations, improvisation is required. 
Before anything else, ensure that all nearby onlookers are out of harm's way and secure the area. Seek the 
nearest Area Director who will contact the Camp Director, Program Director, and the Health Officer if 
necessary. The situation will be assessed and dealt with according to the best judgment of the 
management. 
 
Media  
On occasion, members of the news media may come to camp for a variety of reasons. This may be for a 
simple public interest story or due to emergencies (fire, flood, etc.). In EVERY case, they should be referred 
directly to the Camp Director. Unless authorized, DO NOT answer any questions or give any information. Do 
NOT say "no comment," simply explain that you can't discuss anything with the media. Be respectful and 
courteous to the reporters. Interviews will only be provided through official channels. This is to ensure 
information will not be misrepresented in the story. That said, be friendly and model Scouts as this might be 
your chance to represent Scouting to the public. 
 
 
Active Shooters 
An active shooter event is hard to plan for because unlike most of our emergency procedures, it's not an 
act of nature. If possible, information will be relayed via the radios. Either way, if you hear gunshots, assume 
an active shooter situation. The goal here is to keep you safe, and the scouts safe. Guide those around you 
to hide, find secure shelter, flee into the woods, or fight back directly if necessary or safe to do so. Don't be 
a hero and try to take on the shooter by yourself unless you are absolutely confident in your plan. This is a 
very unlikely scenario, but unfortunately one that must be considered. 
 
Minor Incidents 
 
Minor Property Damage 
As soon as you discover the problem, or if an issue is reported to you, inspect the damage for yourself to 
evaluate if it is an immediate hazard. If it is a dangerous situation, make sure someone guards the area while 
you report it immediately to the Camp Director. If not immediately dangerous, report the damage to the 
Camp Director and, if necessary, the Camp Ranger as soon as possible. If it was an accident on your part, 
please don’t let that prevent you from reporting the issue. Accidents happen, and if no ill intent was 
involved, there’s no need for punishment.   
 
Injury 
Participants are to report to their unit leader, who may either escort the injured party to the Health Lodge. If 
unsure of the extent of the injury or the party seems unable to walk send for first aid through a staff 
member. Staff will evaluate the injured to determine mobility. If, however, you are unsure DO NOT move the 
party but send for first aid. 
 
Major Incidents 
 
40 

 
Major Property Damage  
Do not try to fix it yourself. Document details of the damage and make sure there is no danger to others. 
Report any damage to the Camp Director and the Camp Ranger immediately. An incident report may need 
to be completed. 
 
Injury 
Participants DO NOT move or attempt to move the injured party. Make sure they are not in further danger 
and report to the unit leader, who in turn will locate the first staff member available, report the problem and 
then return to the site to be with the injured party. DO NOT LEAVE THE INJURED PARTY ALONE AT ANY 
TIME. Staff - DO NOT move or attempt to move the injured party. Evaluate the situation to see there is no 
further danger or additional injury and the injured is as comfortable as possible. Send for the first aid 
personnel on duty, stay with the injured party and after first aid has arrived remain as runner and crowd 
control until emergency is over and you are relieved. Make sure the Camp Director and the Camp Ranger 
are notified. 
 
 
 
41

LEGAL POLICIES AND INFORMATION 
 
At Will Employment 
Employment with Catalina Council - Scouting America, is "at-will." This means employees are free to resign 
at any time, with or without cause. Meanwhile, Catalina Council may terminate the employment relationship 
at any time, with or without cause or advance notice. As an at-will employee, There is no guarantee that you 
will be employed with Catalina Council or Camp Lawton for any set period of time.  
 
Equal Employment Opportunity & Your Rights  
Catalina Council - Scouting America is an Equal Opportunity Employer. Employment opportunities at 
Catalina Council are based upon one's qualifications and capabilities to perform the essential functions of a 
particular job. All employment opportunities are provided without regard to race, religion, sex (including 
sexual orientation and transgender status), pregnancy, childbirth or related medical conditions, national 
origin, age, veteran status, disability, genetic information, or any other characteristic protected by law. 
 
This Equal Employment Opportunity policy governs all aspects of employment, including, but not limited to, 
recruitment, hiring, selection, job assignment, promotions, transfers, compensation, discipline, termination, 
layoff, access to benefits and training, and all other conditions and privileges of employment. 
 
Camp Lawton will provide reasonable accommodations as necessary and where required by law so long as 
the accommodation does not pose an undue hardship on the business. Camp Lawton will also 
accommodate sincerely held religious beliefs of its employees to the extent the accommodation does not 
pose an undue hardship on the 
business. If you would like to request an accommodation, or have any questions about your rights and 
responsibilities, contact the Camp Director. This policy is not intended to afford employees with any greater 
protections than those which exist under federal, state or local law. 
 
Catalina Council - Scouting America strongly urges the reporting of all instances of discrimination and 
harassment, and prohibits retaliation against any individual who reports discrimination, harassment, or 
participates in an investigation of such a report. Catalina Council - Scouting America will take appropriate 
disciplinary action, up to and including 
immediate termination, against any employee who violates this policy. 
 
Employee Grievances  
It is the policy of Catalina Council - Scouting America to maintain a harmonious workplace environment. 
Catalina Council encourages its employees to express concerns about work-related issues, including 
workplace communication, interpersonal conflict, and other working conditions. Employees are 
encouraged to raise concerns with their Area Directors.  
 
If not resolved at this level, an employee may submit, in writing, a signed grievance to the Camp Director. 
After receiving a written grievance, Catalina Council may hold a meeting with the employee, the immediate 
supervisor, and any other individuals who may assist in the investigation or resolution of the issue. All 
discussions related to the grievance will be limited to those involved with, and who can assist with, resolving 
the issue. 
 
Complaints involving alleged discriminatory practices shall be processed in accordance with Catalina 
Council's Sexual and other Unlawful Harassment Policy. Catalina Council, assures that all employees filing a 
grievance or complaint can do so without fear of retaliation or reprisal. 
 
Internal Communication 
Effective and ongoing communication within Catalina Council is essential. As such, the Camp Lawton 
maintains systems through which important information can be shared among employees and 
management. Bulletin boards are posted in designated areas around camp to display important information 
and announcements. In addition, Catalina Council uses the Internet and email to facilitate communication 
and share access to documents. To avoid confusion, anything posted or removed from the bulletin boards 
42 

 
must be approved by the Camp or Program Director. All employees are responsible for checking internal 
communications on a frequent and regular basis. Staff should consult their directors with any questions or 
concerns on information disseminated. 
 
Anti-Retaliation and Whistleblower Policy 
This policy is designed to protect employees and address Catalina Council/Camp Lawton's commitment to 
integrity and ethical behavior. In accordance with anti-retaliation and whistleblower protection regulations, 
Camp Lawton will not tolerate any retaliation against an employee who: 
●​
Makes a good faith complaint, or threatens to make a good faith complaint,regarding the 
suspected Organization or employee violations of the law, including discriminatory or other unfair 
employment practices; 
●​
Makes a good faith complaint, or threatens to make a good faith complaint, regarding accounting, 
internal accounting controls, or auditing matters that may lead to incorrect, or misrepresentations 
in, financial accounting;  
●​
Makes a good faith report, or threatens to make a good faith report, of a violation that endangers 
the health or safety of an employee, patient, client or customer, environment or general public; 
●​
Objects to, or refuses to participate in, any activity, policy or practice, which the employee 
reasonably believes is a violation of the law; 
●​
Provides information to assist in an investigation regarding violations of the law; or Files, testifies, 
participates or assists in a proceeding, action or hearing in relation to alleged violations of the law. 
 
Retaliation is defined as any adverse employment action against an employee, including, but not limited to, 
refusal to hire, failure to promote, demotion, suspension, harassment, denial of training opportunities, 
termination, or discrimination in any manner in the terms and conditions of employment. 
 
Anyone found to have engaged in retaliation or in violation of law, policy or practice will be subject to 
discipline, up to and including termination of employment. Employees who knowingly make a false report of 
a violation will be subject to disciplinary action, up to and including termination. 
 
Employees who wish to report a violation should contact their Camp Director directly. Employees should 
also review their state and local requirements for any additional reporting guidelines. Catalina 
Council/Camp Lawton will promptly and thoroughly investigate and, if necessary, address any reported 
violation. Employees who have any questions or concerns regarding this policy and related reporting 
requirements should contact their Camp Director or any state or local agency responsible for investigating 
alleged violations.

part_2_policies_procedures_guidelines_an.camp_opening_procedures:
CAMP OPENING PROCEDURES 
 
3-Year Activity Review Cycle 
Per national policy, the camp administration is required to review all camp activities and programs at least 
every three years.  
 
Program Hazard Analysis (PHA)  
A written analysis of any activity not covered by a standard merit badge pamphlet must be updated every 5 
years. For any safety-critical tasks, including rock climbing activities, a “Call and Response” check (ie “on 
belay” - “belay on”) is required by Scouting America.  
 
Forest Service Permitting 
Catalina Council and the Camp Director are responsible for assuring that our responsibility to our Forest 
Service lease is met. This includes coordinating all major facility work through the Forest Service, proper 
permitting, and banning all paramilitary maneuvers, gambling, and hazardous waste storage.  
 
Pre-Camp Training 
Staff members are required to participate in all council sponsored pre-camp training and orientation 
programs during the spring.  
 
Staff Week 
A week (2-6 days depending on the needs of camp and the discretion of management) of training and 
preparation for camp will precede the first week of camp and is mandatory in its entirety unless other 
arrangements are made with the Camp Director.  
 
Opening Inventory 
At the start of each summer camp season, Area Directors and their staff will do a complete inventory of the 
area’s current supplies and what they need. Keep that inventory updated throughout the summer to make 
the closing inventory easier and to ensure your supplies are replenished efficiently as needed.  
 
Throughout the summer this equipment is under the care of the Area Director and that staff member is 
responsible for its safe use and care. 
 
At the end of summer the director makes a closing inventory with all the supplies you’ll be leaving behind, 
and supplies you recommend for the next year. Any supplies being stored elsewhere should have a record 
of where those are. All camp equipment must be accounted for.  
 
Risk Identification 
Area Directors carefully examine their program areas for any potential hazards. Any hazards should be 
recorded and a plan to correct and/or manage the hazard should be created by the Area Director and camp 
administration. Hazards can include unsafe trails, dangerous trees, bad fencing, broken floors, and unsightly 
aesthetics.

Sure it is, but it’s master content, just curated by me. I’ve dug up information from experts and put together 
what I think is an easy to follow but highly advanced guide to creating genuinely funny skits and songs from 
scratch.  
I mentioned earlier that many of the skits I see today are 50 or more years old. Everyone brings something 
new to them, which is great, but how many more times do I have to see “Is it time yet?” With Youtube easily 
offering over 50 years of Saturday Night Live, Monty Python, Kids in the Hall, Second City, Groundlings, 
Upright Citizens Brigade, even BYU’s Studio C, not to mention countless Tiktok/Instagram Reels/YouTube 
Shorts comedy bits, there’s really no excuse for falling back on the same old jokes.   
 
But imagine if the next great skit, came from YOU! There’s no reason you can’t write a sketch all by yourself 
that’s funny, appropriate, and topical.

part_3_campfire_master_class_and_songboo.how_to_write_funny:
How To Write Funny 
“It’s said that a comic is a person who says funny things but a comedian says things funny. This makes me a 
juggler.” -Michael Davis  
 
Sources: How To Write Funny by Scott Dikkers; Laughing Matters (video) Starring Rowan Atkinson, written by 
David Hinton, Robin Driscoll, and Rowan Atkinson; The Upright Citizens Brigade Comedy Improvisation 
Manual by Matt Walsh, Ian Roberts, and Matt Besser 
Module 1: Finding Your Core Concept 
●​
Generate Ideas Constantly: Make a daily habit of brainstorming at least 10 funny ideas or short 
jokes to build a stockpile of raw material. 
●​
The "First Laugh" Test: A good skit starts with a winning concept that can be expressed as a simple 
5–10 word title. Test your ideas on a feedback group; if the premise doesn’t get an immediate "First 
Laugh," it won't work as a full skit. 
●​
Establish One Impossible Thing: Your skit should introduce a unique comedy world containing only 
one element that is askew from reality. If you introduce too many crazy elements, the audience will 
get confused. 
●​
Include Hidden Subtext: Ensure your concept is grounded in an astute observation or opinion about 
human nature or society (Subtext), but never explicitly state this message to the audience. 
Module 2: Establishing the Scene and the "Take" 
●​
Build the Base Reality: Before any comedy happens, establish the "Who, What, and Where" of the 
scene. Ground the scene in a relatable, normal context so the absurd elements have something to 
contrast against. 
●​
Find the Game of the Scene (The Take): Identify the "first unusual thing" that breaks the normal 
pattern of your base reality. This unusual behavior or absurd situation becomes your "take" or the 
"game"—the single specific idea that makes the skit funny. 
Module 3: Brainstorming and Joke Discipline 
●​
Ask "If/Then": To generate the comedy for your skit, ask yourself: "If this unusual thing is true, then 
what else is true?". 
●​
Riff "Joke Beats": Brainstorm a list of 20 or more joke beats that spool out from your core concept. 
These beats will form the skeleton of your skit, occurring every few lines. 
●​
Maintain Joke Discipline: You must ruthlessly cut any jokes that veer away from your core premise, 
no matter how funny they are on their own. Derailing from the established "track" is a common 
beginner mistake that confuses audiences. 
Module 4: Crafting Comedic Characters 
46 

 
●​
Use Simple Archetypes: Comedic characters are not meant to be deep or realistic; they are 
two-dimensional representations of human flaws. Rely on proven Character Archetypes (like the 
Trickster, the Bumbling Authority, or the Dummy) and give them just 1–3 clear traits. 
●​
Let Traits Drive the Dialogue: Don't write random, clever "gags" for your characters to say. Humor 
naturally arises simply by having the characters act and speak in strict accordance with their 
Archetypal traits. 
●​
Play it Straight: Characters must fully commit to their absurd reality and react truthfully (playing at 
the "top of their intelligence"). Avoid winking at the audience, acting "wacky," or using silly, cliché 
character names just for a cheap laugh. 
Module 5: Escalation and Drafting 
●​
Escalate the Humor: Your skit must build momentum. Every new joke beat should escalate the 
stakes, heightening the scene to be bigger, crazier, and more absurd than the last. 
●​
Fold in the Exposition: Do not write long, humorless setups to explain the logic of your scene. 
Integrate necessary exposition seamlessly into your joke beats. 
●​
Enter "Clown Mode": When writing your first draft, turn off your inner critic. String your joke beats 
together quickly and finish the draft without second-guessing or editing yourself. 
Module 6: The Closer and the Edit 
●​
Cap it with a Button: End the skit with a "closer" or a "button"—a final, definitive joke that wraps up 
the scene. This is the only place in the skit where you are allowed to slightly break the internal logic 
or flip the premise upside down for a final surprise. 
●​
Enter "Editor Mode": Put the draft away for a few days, then return with an objective eye. Cut any 
needless words to keep the pacing tight. 
●​
Test and Rewrite: Show the completed draft to your feedback group or test it on a real audience. If 
the joke beats don't land or the audience gets confused, be prepared to scrap parts of it and 
rewrite until it works.
part_3_campfire_master_class_and_songboo.writing_songs:
Writing Songs 
When the Llama Song was written, it began with the 1994 Camp Lawton Cub Scout Camp staff having Na 
Na Hey Hey (Kiss Him Goodbye) (Steam, 1969) stuck in their heads. That just naturally evolved into “Llama 
llama - I ride a - ride a llama.” Amidst giggles over the brilliance of that wordplay, staff member Morgan 
Grantham went away for a bit and wrote the lyrics to the verses as a rap which later became the tune to We 
Will Rock You (Queen, 1977). That's how great songs are written sometimes. It's just a matter of finding the 
hook. 
 
When you're leading a troop around the campfire or trying to keep energy up on the logs, you don't have 
the luxury of a backing track or a synthesizer. The song has to be entirely driven by the group's collective 
energy. 
Here is a cheat sheet for writing a camp song that meets your needs: it demands participation, teaches 
itself, and sticks in the head long after the fire burns out. 
 
The Melodic Blueprint 
●​
The Staircase Melody: Keep the core melody restricted to a tight range of notes that move up and 
down in small, predictable steps. If the notes jump around too frantically, it becomes difficult for an 
untrained group to sing acapella. 
●​
The "Millennial Whoop" Equivalent: You need a wordless, rhythmic vocalization. The "Woo-oo!" in 
DuckTales. Camp songs thrive on these nonsensical, rhythmic syllables (think "Boom Chicka Boom" 
or "Flea Fly Flow"). They are universally accessible, require zero memorization, and immediately 
unify the group's timing. 
47 

 
 
Structuring for "Zero-Friction" Learning 
●​
 The "One at a Time" Rule: Borrow pop producer Max Martin’s philosophy: never introduce too 
many elements at once. If the melody is complex, keep the lyrics dead simple. If the lyrics tell a 
detailed story, keep the melody to a basic chant. 
●​
Call and Response (The Echo): The absolute easiest way to teach a song without accompaniment 
is the "Repeat After Me" format. You sing a line, the group echoes it. By the time you reach the 
chorus, they are already trained on the rhythm and key. 
●​
The Zipper Structure: Design the verses so that only one word changes each time. For example, 
"We're going on a bear hunt" simply zippers in a new obstacle (mud, river, grass) while the rest of 
the framework remains identical. This minimizes the cognitive load on the singers. 
 
Writing Lyrics that Stick 
●​
Bookend Rhymes: Rhyming at both the beginning and the end of phrases makes lyrics significantly 
more memorable. It gives the brain a predictive puzzle to solve, making the next line feel inevitable. 
●​
Action-Oriented Verbs: Camp songs need kinetic energy. Use words that easily translate to 
physical movements. If the song mentions rain, there should be a corresponding hand motion. This 
taps into kinesthetic learning, helping people remember the words through muscle memory. 
●​
Escalation: A great camp song builds tension or absurdity. It should get faster, louder, or 
fundamentally weirder with each verse until it culminates in a high-energy, chaotic finale. 
 
Designing the Acapella "Track" 
●​
Body Percussion: Since you have no instruments, the arrangement must include built-in 
percussion. Dictate specific moments for stomps, claps, or lap-slaps. This serves a dual purpose: it 
keeps the tempo from dragging (a common problem in acapella group singing) and provides an 
easy participation avenue for those who might be too shy to sing loudly at first. 
 
 
 
48 

 
Songbook 
 
I spent years collecting songs for a songbook I’d one day publish. Perhaps now my time has come! 
Well, not really. For one, I don’t want to make anyone print a 300 page document. Also, many of them don’t 
fit the NCS Guidelines we discussed earlier. Finally, well… 
 
Here’s one last lesson on the law making things inconventient 
Copyright. The reality is, being a summer camp does not protect us from copyright. Legally, printing songs 
in songbooks is copying and publishing copyright material and singing the songs in camp is  life 
performance. There’s not many ways around it, and being a summer camp doesn’t legally give us any leway. 
We can get permission and/or license the songs, but that’s an awful lot of work you’re probably not going to 
do. There’s some wiggle room when it comes to fair use, but you’d want to have a chat with a lawyer. 
 
However, the chances of a company like Disney going after Camp Lawton for singing a song in good faith 
are probably about as high as winning the lottery or getting struck my lightning (while following our 
hazardous weather guidelines). Realistically, don’t worry about it. BUT… let’s not tempt fate by putting it in 
writing.

CAMP LAWTON SUMMER CAMP STAFF 
COMMITMENT & CODE OF CONDUCT 
The Scout Oath and Law are the foundation of our camp culture. As a Camp Lawton staff member, your 
personal habits and actions are the living embodiment of Scouting. You agree to align your conduct, both 
on and off the clock, with the ideals of Scouting America, ensuring you remain a positive role model and 
maintain the physical and mental readiness required to serve our campers. 
To maintain a safe, highly functional, and supportive camp environment, we rely on a shared standard of 
behavior. 
Our Support & Accountability Framework 
We recognize that working at camp is a high-pressure environment. Mistakes happen, and our primary goal 
is to help you grow and succeed. However, consistent failure to meet standards negatively impacts the 
campers and your fellow staff. We address behavioral and performance issues through a progressive 
support model: 
●​
Phase 1: Coaching & Realignment (The Check-In): For initial or minor lapses in judgment, 
appearance, or performance. Your Area Director will discuss the issue with you, identify any 
underlying causes (e.g., fatigue, lack of training), and clearly outline the expected change. 
●​
Phase 2: Formal Intervention (The Action Plan): For repeated issues or more significant 
breaches of conduct. You will meet with camp administration to create a documented plan for 
improvement. At this stage, youth staff will have their parents or guardians notified. 
●​
Phase 3: Separation (Dismissal): If the intervention plan is not followed, or if a staff member 
proves fundamentally incompatible with the camp's culture and expectations, their employment will 
be terminated. 
Daily Behavioral Expectations 
Rather than listing every possible mistake, staff are expected to proactively uphold the following standards: 
1. Professionalism & Engagement 
●​
Be Present: Arrive on time to all meals, assemblies, and program areas. Sleeping on the job, leaving 
your assigned area without permission, or abandoning your duties creates a safety hazard and 
places an unfair burden on your team. 
●​
Stay Sharp: Maintain excellent personal hygiene, adhere to the uniform guidelines at all times, and 
keep your living quarters and assigned facilities clean and organized. 
●​
Respect the Hierarchy: Follow the instructions of your supervisors and the camp administration. If 
you disagree with a directive, comply first (unless it is unsafe) and use the open-door grievance 
policy to discuss it afterward. 
2. Respect for the Community 
●​
Assume Positive Intent: Treat everyone with the courtesy and kindness outlined in the Scout Law. 
Abusive language, obscene gestures, bullying, hazing, and malicious pranks have no place at Camp 
Lawton. 
57 

 
●​
Maintain Boundaries: Respect the privacy of others. Never enter another cabin without 
permission, and strictly adhere to all Safeguarding Youth guidelines regarding one-on-one contact 
and appropriate supervision. 
●​
Honor the Environment: Care for camp equipment and property as if it were your own. 
Unauthorized use of camp materials or driving vehicles in camp without express permission from 
the Ranger and Camp Director is prohibited. 
3. Integrity & Focus 
●​
Media Standards: Any media consumed in camp must align with Scouting values. Staff may not 
view R-rated movies or play M-rated video games on camp property. 
●​
Financial Integrity: Staff may not accept gratuities, bribes, or tips from campers or visitors, nor 
may they sell personal items or services on camp property. 
●​
Authorized Visitors: All visitors must be approved in advance by the Camp Director and follow all 
sign-in procedures to ensure camp security. 
Zero-Tolerance: Immediate Discharge 
While we prioritize coaching, certain actions pose an immediate threat to the physical, emotional, or legal 
safety of our campers, staff, and organization. Involvement in any of the following will result in immediate 
dismissal, removal from camp property, and, when applicable, reporting to law enforcement and the 
Department of Child Safety: 
1.​ Safety & Abuse Violations: Any violation of Safeguarding Youth policies, including sexual or 
physical harassment, abuse, hazing, or any sexual activities on camp property. 
2.​ Substance Violations: The possession, use, or being under the influence of alcohol, illicit drugs, or 
unprescribed medications. (Smoking or vaping by anyone under 21 is strictly prohibited; authorized 
use by those 21+ is heavily restricted).

5.​ Falsification: Misrepresenting information on your application, medical forms, or official incident

6.​ Endangerment: Transporting a minor in a vehicle without written parental permission filed directly
